content.title: 2022 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease
content.description: A report of the American Heart Association and American College of Cardiology Joint Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines. Developed in collaboration with and endorsed by several professional bodies related to cardiac care.
writingCommittee: Eric M. Isselbacher, MD, MSc, FACC, Chair
Ourania Preventza, MD, MBA, Vice Chair
James Hamilton Black III, MD, DSVS, Vice Chair
Tyrone J. Collins, MD, MSCAI, FACC, FAHA, FSVVM
Caitlin W. Hicks, MD, MS, FSVS
Y. Joseph Woo, MD, FACC, FAHA
content.endorsement: Endorsed by the Society of Interventional Radiology and Society for Vascular Medicine.

genes.context: Dianna M. Milewicz, MD, PhD

content.AIM: The '2022 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease' provides recommendations to guide clinicians in the diagnosis, genetic evaluation and family screening, medical therapy, endovascular and surgical treatment, and long-term surveillance of patients with aortic disease across its multiple clinical presentation subsets (i.e., asymptomatic, stable symptomatic, and acute aortic syndromes).
content.METHODS: A comprehensive literature search was conducted from January 2021 to April 2021, encompassing studies, reviews, and other evidence conducted on human subjects that were published in English from PubMed, EMBASE, the Cochrane Library, CINAHL Complete, and other selected databases relevant to this guideline. Additional relevant studies, published through June 2022 during the guideline writing process, were also considered by the writing committee, where appropriate.
content.STRUCTURE: Recommendations from previously published AHA/ACC guidelines on thoracic aortic disease, peripheral artery disease, and bicuspid aortic valve disease have been updated with new evidence to guide clinicians. In addition, new recommendations addressing comprehensive care for patients with aortic disease have been developed. There is added emphasis on the role of shared decision making, especially in the management of patients with aortic disease both before and during pregnancy. There is also an increased emphasis on the importance of institutional interventional volume and multidisciplinary aortic team expertise in the care of patients with aortic disease.

key_messages: Care for aortic disease patients is enhanced in centers with high volumes and expert teams.
Shared decision-making is crucial for pregnant patients and those contemplating pregnancy.
Imaging recommendations for aortic disease include CT, MRI, and echocardiography.
Surgical intervention thresholds vary based on specific conditions such as sporadic or heritable aortic aneurysms.
Valve-sparing aortic root replacement can be suitable during replacement surgery.
High-volume centers are recommended for patients with acute type A aortic dissection.
Increasing use of endovascular aortic repair for type B aortic dissection.

content.Introduction: Since 1980, the American College of Cardiology (ACC) and American Heart Association (AHA) have translated scientific evidence into clinical practice guidelines to improve cardiovascular health.
content.Guideline Evolution: These guidelines are based on systematic methods to evaluate and classify evidence, providing a foundation for quality cardiovascular care delivery. The ACC and AHA develop and publish these guidelines without commercial support.
content.Intended Use: Clinical practice guidelines are intended for patients at risk of developing cardiovascular disease, relevant to global practices, aiming to improve patient care quality and interest.
content.Clinical Implementation: Effective management requires both practitioners and patients to adhere. Shared decision-making is emphasized for personalized patient care.
content.Methodology and Modernization: The AHA/ACC Joint Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines continuously reviews guidelines based on published standards and evolving technologies.
content.Guideline Format: Guidelines are presented in a 'knowledge chunk' format, containing recommendations and supportive text. They are reviewed dynamically to ensure relevancy.
content.Selection of Committee Members: Members are selected to represent diverse expertise and perspectives, ensuring broad cardiovascular knowledge.

content.introduction: This guideline from the ACC and AHA focuses on methodologies and evidence review for aortic disease. It emphasizes the importance of using evidence-based recommendations and includes exhaustive literature reviews. The guideline is transparent about relationships with industry and follows rigorous processes for developing recommendations.
content.methodology_and_evidence_review: The recommendations are based on evidence found using comprehensive literature reviews. Searches involved databases like MEDLINE and focused on topics such as aortic occlusion, aortic aneurysm, diagnostic imaging, and various types of surveillance. A systematic approach assesses both primary and secondary sources for relevance and strength.
content.organization_of_the_writing_committee: The writing committee includes a diverse group of professionals such as cardiologists, radiologists, and representatives from various cardiovascular and surgical societies.
content.guideline_directed_medical_therapy: This includes clinical evaluations, diagnostic testing, and recommended treatment regimens. Recommendations focus on ensuring patient safety and effectiveness, limited to those approved in the U.S.

content.Class of Recommendations and Level of Ev: The Class of Recommendation (COR) indicates the strength of recommendation. The Level of Evidence (LOE) rates the quality of scientific evidence.

levels_of_evidence.level_a: High-quality evidence from more than 1 RCT or meta-analyses of high-quality RCTs
levels_of_evidence.level_b-r: Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more RCTs or meta-analyses of moderate-quality RCTs
levels_of_evidence.level_b-nr: Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more well-designed, well-executed nonrandomized studies, observational studies, or registry studies or meta-analyses of such studies
levels_of_evidence.level_c-ld: Randomized or nonrandomized observational or registry studies with limitations of design or execution, meta-analyses of such studies, physiological or mechanistic studies in human subjects
levels_of_evidence.level_c-eo: Consensus of expert opinion based on clinical experience

content.Normal Aortic Anatomy: The aorta is the largest artery in the body, divided into 5 main segments: root, ascending thoracic aorta, aortic arch, descending thoracic aorta, and abdominal aorta.
content.Aortic Wall Composition: Comprised of 3 layers: intima, media, adventitia. The media contains smooth muscle cells, elastic fibers, collagen proteins, and polysaccharides with elastic lamellae.
content.Aortic Landing Zones: Classifies aortic anatomy to guide aortic interventions. The aorta is divided into 11 landing zones influenced by proximal sealing during endovascular procedures.
content.Definitions of Dilation and Aneurysm: An aneurysm is when an artery is dilated to at least 1.5 times its normal diameter, applicable to abdominal and descending thoracic aorta. The guideline for diagnosing aneurysms in the aortic root and ascending thoracic aorta considers a diameter of ≥1.5 times reference diameter.

content.content: The guideline image illustrates the anatomy of the aorta and its main branches, including the right and left common carotid arteries, right and left subclavian arteries, innominate (brachiocephalic) artery, ascending thoracic aorta, aortic arch, descending thoracic aorta, aortic root (sinuses of Valsalva), sinotubular junction, aortic annulus, diaphragm, suprarenal abdominal aorta, superior mesenteric artery, celiac axis, right and left renal arteries, inferior mesenteric artery, right and left common iliac arteries, and the abdominal aorta. The diagram is part of the 2022 ACC/AHA aortic disease guideline focusing on aortic anatomy.

content.diagram: A simplified diagram depicts the key histologic components of the aortic wall, including Adventitia, Media, Intima, Smooth muscle, Elastic lamellae, Vasa vasorum, Internal elastic membrane, External elastic membrane, Nervi vasorum, and Endothelium.
content.clinical considerations: Aortic repair is recommended for diameters of ≤5.0 cm to prevent aneurysm. Diameter thresholds for dilated aorta or aneurysm are based on the natural history of abnormal aortas. Studies show different levels of risk for dissection and aneurysm at various aortic diameters. Risk increases significantly with diameters ≥4.0 cm.
content.research findings: Borger et al studied patients with bicuspid aortic valve undergoing aortic valve replacement and found increased risk with baseline aortic diameters of 4.5 cm to 4.9 cm. Paruchuri et al analyzed data from the MESA study to estimate risk of dissection.
content.definitions: The term 'dilated ascending aorta' is defined as ≥4.0 cm, consistent with European Society of Cardiology guidelines. 'Dilation' is preferred to 'ectasia' in clinical settings.

Graphs.Title: Freedom From Ascending Aortic Complications for Patients With Bicuspid Aortic Valve Disease
Graphs.Description: The chart shows the percentage of patients free from composite outcomes related to aortic complications postoperatively over a span of 15 years.
Findings: Patients with an aortic diameter <4.0 mm have a 78% ± 6% freedom from complications at 15 years.
Patients with an aortic diameter 4.0 - 4.5 mm have an 81% ± 6% freedom from complications at 15 years.

Graphs.Title: Relative Risk of Aortic Dissection by Size Range
Graphs.Description: The graph represents the relative risk of aortic dissection in relation to the size of the aorta in centimeters.
Findings: Relative risk starts to rise significantly at an aortic diameter of 4.0 cm and above.
Significantly higher risk is observed at diameters ≥4.5 cm, with a relative risk value of 6,305.5.

Risk Assessment: Patients with moderate dilation of the ascending aorta (4.5 cm-4.9 cm) have a significantly increased risk of future aortic complications, such as aneurysm, dissection, or sudden death.
Ratio Interpretation: A cross-sectional area to height ratio of >10 cm²/m was initially used as a threshold for increased risk and need for intervention.
Recent guidelines suggest a simpler ratio of ≥10 cm²/m as a critical threshold for predicting risk.

content.Definitions and Classification of AAS: Acute Aortic Syndromes (AAS) are life-threatening conditions caused by breaches in the integrity of the aortic wall, including aortic dissection, intramural hematoma, and penetrating atherosclerotic ulcer.
content.Aortic Dissection: Occurs when a tear in the intimal layer allows blood to pass, creating a false lumen. It can lead to complications like aortic regurgitation, myocardial ischemia, cardiac tamponade, stroke, or malperfusion syndromes. If the false lumen tears externally, it can be fatal.
content.Incidence and Demographics: Aortic dissection occurs in 5 to 30 cases per million per year, primarily affecting men aged 50 to 70. Conditions like Marfan syndrome, BAV, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, and vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome are risk factors.
content.Temporal Classification: Dissections are classified into hyperacute (<24 hours), acute (2-7 days), subacute (8-30 days), and chronic (>30 days).

genes.context: Patients with conditions like Marfan syndrome, BAV, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, and vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome present at younger ages.

content.guideline: Classification of Aortic Dissection Chronically Based on the 2020 SVS/STS Reporting Standards.

classification.notes: The DeBakey system offers greater anatomic detail, distinguishing dissections involving ascending thoracic aorta from those that do not, whereas the Stanford system is simpler.

mortality_info.early_mortality: 1% to 2% per hour after symptom onset for acute aortic dissection.
mortality_info.acute_type_b_mortality: 30-day mortality rate of 10%, increasing to 20% by day 2 and 25% by day 30 if complications arise.

debakey_system.description: Dissection tear originates in the ascending aorta and propagates distally to include the aortic arch and typically the descending aorta.
Dissection tear is confined only to the ascending aorta.
Dissection tear originates in the descending thoracic aorta and propagates most often distally.

subtype.description: Dissection tear is confined only to the descending thoracic aorta.
Dissection tear originates in the descending thoracic aorta and extends below the diaphragm.

stanford_classification.description: All dissections involving the ascending aorta, irrespective of the site of the intimal tear.
All dissections that do not involve the ascending aorta (including dissections that involve the aortic arch but spare the ascending aorta).

anatomic_classification.ad: Indicates type A for any dissection with an entry tear in zone 0 and extends distally.
anatomic_classification.bpd: Type B is used for any dissection with an entry tear in zone 1 or beyond.
anatomic_classification.id: When a dissection begins in zone 0 but the location of the entry tear has not been identified.

content.malperfusion_syndrome: Occurs when there is end-organ ischemia related to inadequate perfusion of the aortic is~~

2.4.2.Title: Intramural Hematoma
2.4.2.Description: IMH describes the presence of blood within the medial layer of the aortic wall in the absence of an overt intimal tear or patent false lumen. The blood may arise from vasa vasorum bleeding or small intimal tears not seen on standard imaging.
2.4.2.Diagnosis: Diagnosed by computed tomographic angiography (CTA), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and echocardiography by presence of a circular or crescent-shaped thickening of the aortic wall >5 mm.
2.4.2.Statistics: 5% to 25% of patients have IMH, with a high prevalence of 30% to 40% in Asian literature.

2.4.3.Title: Penetrating Atherosclerotic Ulcer
2.4.3.Description: PAU begins with ulceration of an atherosclerotic plaque, allowing blood into the medial layer; often seen in older patients (>70 years of age).
2.4.3.Incidence: Accounts for 2% to 7% of acute aortic syndromes.

content.classification_of_thoracoabdominal_aorti: The classification of thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysms is based on the extent of aortic involvement. Extent I involves the area below the left subclavian to above the celiac axis. Extent II involves the left subclavian including infra-renal abdominal aorta to the iliac bifurcation. Extent III involves below T6 intercostal space, tapering to just above infra-renal abdominal aorta to the iliac bifurcation. Extent IV involves below T12, tapering to above the iliac bifurcation. Extent V involves below T6, tapering to just above the renal arteries.
content.additional_comorbidities: Common comorbidities include hypertension, tobacco use, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and renal insufficiency.
content.management_of_pau: The management strategy of PAU should be individualized, considering the clinical presentation, imaging features, and patient's comorbidities. The risk of rupture can be up to 40%.
content.classification_of_endoLeaks: Endovascular stent-grafting is a common repair method for aortic aneurysms, but endografting has limitations like potential endoleaks.

content.Endoleaks Overview: Endoleaks are classified into five types based on the source and location of the leak. They result in the persistence of blood flow outside the graft and within the aneurysm sac, hindering complete thrombosis. Lifelong surveillance imaging is necessary to monitor the appearance of endoleaks.

Types of Endoleaks.Type II: Backfilling of the aneurysm sac through branch vessels of the aorta.
Types of Endoleaks.Type IV: Leakage through the graft wall attributable to endograft porosity.
Types of Endoleaks.Type V: Caused by endotension, possibly resulting from aortic pressure transmitted through the graft/thrombus to the aneurysm sac.

content.Patient Management: Patients with endografts require lifelong surveillance to monitor for the occurrence of endoleaks.

Imaging and Measurements.Techniques: Aortic imaging techniques recommended for patients with known or suspected aortic disease.
Recommendations: Measure aortic diameters at reproducible anatomic landmarks perpendicular to axis of blood flow.
Maintain low ionizing radiation doses.
Use CT or MR imaging to measure aortic diameters from inner-edge to inner-edge.
Report aortic root diameter as maximum sinus to sinus measurement.
Index dilated root or ascending aorta to patient height or BSA.
Measure aorta using echocardiography from leading-edge to leading-edge.

Optimized Imaging.Technology: 3D imaging data sets for structured measurement.

Essential Elements of CT and MRI Aortic: Maximum aortic diameter at each level of dilation, perpendicular to the axis of blood flow. In cases of asymmetric or oval contour, the longest diameter and its perpendicular diameter should be reported. Standard measurement levels may be included, even when normal.
Wall changes suggestive of atherosclerosis, diffuse thickening (eg, aortitis), or mural thrombus.
Evidence of luminal stenosis/occlusion, including location, severity, and length.

Findings suggestive of acute aortic syndrome (eg, communicating dissection, intramural hematoma, penetrating atherosclerotic ulcer, focal intimal tear), including proximal/distal extension, suspected entry tear site (if visible), and complications (eg, active contrast extravasation, rupture, contained rupture including pericardic hemorrhage, pericardial and pleural fluid, mediastinal stranding).
Extension of aortic disease process (acute or chronic) into branch vessels, findings suggestive of end-organ injury, and suspected malperfusion.
Direct comparison with previous examinations should be detailed to identify pertinent changes.
Presence and extent of repair (eg, interposition graft, endovascular stent graft), as well as any evidence of complication.
Impression regarding disease classification (eg, acute aortic syndrome, aneurysm/pseudoaneurysm, luminal stenosis, atherosclerotic aortic disease).

Relevant details regarding method of image acquisition (eg, use of electrocardiographic-gating and phase of acquisition) and measurement (eg, axial versus double oblique, inner-edge versus outer-edge) should be included.

content.General Information: The guideline discusses imaging techniques for assessing aortic disease progression. Techniques such as echocardiography, MRI, and CT are used to measure different parts of the aorta.

Measurement Techniques.Echocardiography: Leading-edge to leading-edge measurement for assessing aortic root, sinotubular junction, and ascending aorta.
Measurement Techniques.Sinus Measurement: Inner-wall to inner-wall measurements, with focus on consistency for MRI and CT.
Measurement Techniques.MRI and CT: Standard sites for measurement using inner-wall techniques for accuracy in interval surveillance.

Clinical Relevance.Measurement Consistency: Consistency in measurement across imaging modalities is necessary for accurate surveillance.
Clinical Relevance.Clinical Implications: Cross-sectional aortic area to patient height ratio correlates with risk of aortic dissection and death in specific valve abnormalities.

content.Historical Context: Data from TTE measurement provide insight into prevalence and importance of aortic dilation, with good correlation to other imaging measurements.

content.Conventions of Measurements: Reproducible and accurate measurements of the aorta are critical for characterizing aortic disease and guiding treatment decisions. Measurements should be obtained perpendicular to the long axis of the aorta at specified segmental locations, taking into account any abnormalities. There is no standardized method for aortic diameter measurements across imaging modalities. Historical data using TTE helps measure the aortic root by identifying the largest diameter, aiding risk stratification and treatment guidance. CT and MRI diameter measurements from sinus to sinus or inner edge to inner edge are correlated with TTE measurements, improving confidence in aortic root imaging.
content.Clinical Observations: Although aortic dilation is a known risk factor for aortic dissection and rupture, most dissections occur in aortas not meeting the preventive surgery diameter threshold. Aortic area indexed to height relates to dissection risk, especially in those with valve concerns. Aortic length increases over time, and excessive elongation may predict dissection, suggesting new potential metrics for risk evaluation.

Computed Tomography.Description: CT can image the entire aorta and its branches with high spatial resolution and fast acquisition.
Computed Tomography.Benefits: High sensitivity and specificity for acute aortic syndromes, coronary involvement, and aortic aneurysms.

Considerations.Iodinated Contrast: Allows delineation between aortic lumen and wall but may cause allergic reactions or renal toxicity.
Considerations.Recent Consensus: The risk of acute kidney injury from contrast is low in patients with impaired renal function.

Magnetic Resonance Imaging.Description: MRI provides coverage of the entire aorta and branch vessels, assessing aortic wall changes.
Magnetic Resonance Imaging.Benefits: No ionizing radiation, can assess congenital systemic fibrosis risk.
Magnetic Resonance Imaging.Limitations: Inferior spatial resolution to CT, cannot use with patients carrying metallic devices.

Echocardiography.Transthoracic Echocardiography (TTE): Most common imaging modality for thoracic aorta, useful for imaging aortic root.
Echocardiography.Benefits: Portable and valuable for evaluating aortic valve regurgitation and ventricular dysfunction.

content.Transesophageal Echocardiography (TEE): TEE provides high-resolution images of most of the thoracic aorta, aiding in the evaluation of valve anatomy and function. It is useful in perioperative assessment during aortic repair.
content.Intravascular Ultrasound: This technology allows high-resolution imaging for endovascular management, helpful for detecting aortic pathologies and guiding treatment.
content.Abdominal Ultrasound: Recommended for detecting and monitoring AAA (abdominal aortic aneurysm), useful in detecting various aortic pathologies. B-mode imaging, color Doppler, and waveform analysis enhance evaluation.
content.Multidisciplinary Aortic Teams: Evidence-based standards emphasize the correlation between hospital volume and outcomes, highlighting the importance of a comprehensive, multidisciplinary approach for optimal treatment.

content.description: The image discusses the importance of multidisciplinary aortic teams in managing complex aortic diseases. It highlights the need for a team with expertise in cardiac, vascular, and endovascular surgery, as well as imaging specialists and anesthesiologists experienced in cerebrospinal fluid drainage. The text emphasizes the decline in operative mortality associated with larger institutional case volumes and the implementation of multidisciplinary teams.
recommendations: Acknowledge the role of collaborative multidisciplinary teams in cardiovascular care, including heart transplant and mechanical circulatory support.
Highlight the decline in operative mortality and long-term mortality benefits following the use of multidisciplinary teams in aortic surgeries.
studies_referenced: The 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of the Patient With Valvular Heart Disease
Andersen et al's study comparing outcomes for patients with aortic dissection before and after implementation of a multidisciplinary thoracic aortic surgery program

content.title: 2022 ACC/AHA Aortic Disease Guideline
content.summary: The guideline discusses mortality rates associated with aortic surgeries, emphasizing the importance of high-volume centers and expert surgical teams. Data from various studies highlight that mortality is lower in centers with more cases handled annually, especially for urgent aortic dissection repairs. The guideline recommends that certain surgeries be performed in centers with experienced multidisciplinary teams.
keyPoints: High-volume centers report lower mortality rates for aortic surgeries.
Surgical expertise, especially in aortic procedures, significantly affects outcomes.
Aortic specialists perform procedures more frequently and with shorter cross-clamp times.
The guideline underscores the necessity of performing surgeries in centers with multidisciplinary aortic teams.

content.Shared Decision-Making: Shared decision-making is recommended for patients with aortic disease to determine intervention thresholds, surgical repair types, and in medical management and surveillance. It is also vital for patients contemplating pregnancy, especially when considering cardiovascular risks and prophylactic aortic surgery.
content.Aneurysms: Thoracic Aortic Aneurysms occur in 5 to 10 per 100,000 persons. The natural history depends on the cause and location, with risk factors including age, sex, height, body size, hypertension, smoking, hypercholesterolemia, and heritable genetic variants. Patients with TAA have increased incidence of AAA and cerebral aneurysms.

genes.context: Marfan syndrome is associated with aortic root aneurysm, aortic dissection, TAA, MVP, long bone overgrowth, arachnodactyly, dolichostenomelia, scoliosis, pectus deformities, ectopia lentis, myopia, tall stature, pneumothorax, dural ectasia.

Loeys-Dietz syndrome includes TAA, branch vessel aneurysms, aortic dissection, arterial tortuosity, MVP, craniosynostosis, hypertelorism, bluish sclera, bifid/ broad uvula, translucent skin, visible veins, club feet, dural ectasia, and premature osteoarthritis and peripheral neuropathy.
Loeys-Dietz syndrome and BAV with TAA are associated with the gene TGFBR2.
Loeys-Dietz syndrome involves SMAD3 and includes TAA, branch vessel aneurysms, and premature osteoarthritis and peripheral neuropathy.

Vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is associated with COL3A1 and features TAA, AAA, arterial rupture, aortic dissection, MVP, bowel and uterine rupture, pneumothorax, translucent skin, atrophic scars, small joint hypermobility, easy bruising, carotid-cavernous fistula.
Arterial tortuosity syndrome, associated with SLC2A10, involves tortuous large and medium-sized arteries, aortic dilation, craniofacial, skin and skeletal features.
Shprintzen-Goldberg syndrome, associated with SKI, includes craniosynostosis, skeletal features, aortic dilation.

Associated Conditions: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome with periventricular nodular heterotopia
genes.context: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome with periventricular nodular heterotopia associated with FLNA includes X-linked features such as PDA, VSD, seizures, joint hypermobility.
Meester-Loeys syndrome, associated with BGN, involves X-linked features like TAA, aortic dissection, MV disease.
LOX-related TAA features TAA, BAV, aortic dissection, Marfanoid habitus in some.

Associated with smooth muscle dysfunction syndrome, ACTA2 leads to TAA, moyamoya-like cerebrovascular disease, pulmonary hypertension, pulmonary disease, hypoperistalsis, hypotonic bladder, congenital mydriasis. Also involved in nonsyndromic HTAD and familial TAAA.
MYH11 association with familial TAAA involves TAA, aortic dissection, PDA.
MYLK association with familial TAAA involves TAA and aortic dissection at relatively small aortic size.
PRKG1 association with familial TAAA involves aortic dissection at young ages at small aortic sizes.
MAT2A is associated with BAV with TAA and presents in syndromic and nonsyndromic HTAD and FTAA with increased frequency of BAV.
GATA5 is associated with BAV with TAA and presents in syndromic and nonsyndromic HTAD and FTAA with increased frequency of BAV.
SMAD6 is associated with BAV with TAA and presents in syndromic and nonsyndromic HTAD and FTAA with increased frequency of BAV.
LOX is associated with BAV with TAA across syndromic and nonsyndromic HTAD and FTAA presentations.
ROBO4 is associated with BAV with TAA and presents in syndromic and nonsyndromic HTAD and FTAA with increased frequency of BAV.
TBX20 is associated with BAV with TAA and presents in syndromic and nonsyndromic HTAD and FTAA with increased frequency of BAV.

content.General Information: The document discusses the differentiation between sporadic and degenerative thoracic aortic aneurysms (TAA) and the hereditary aspects of aortic root and ascending aortic aneurysms. It highlights the importance of genetic testing and counseling for hereditary aortic diseases, particularly given the significant difference in age of diagnosis between root/ascending and descending aortic aneurysms. The recommendations for genetic testing focus on identifying pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants and include obtaining multigenerational family histories, genetic counseling, and cascade testing for at-risk relatives. The document also emphasizes the importance of recording aortic measurements in cases of acute type A aortic dissection.
Recommendations: Obtain a multigenerational family history for patients with aortic root/ascending aortic aneurysms or aortic dissection.
Conduct genetic testing to identify pathogenic variants for patients with aortic aneurysms or dissection and risk factors for hereditary thoracic aortic diseases (HTAD).
Provide genetic counseling to patients with a pathogenic variant in genes predisposing to HTAD.
Perform genetic testing for at-risk biological relatives of patients with pathogenic variants.
If genetic testing is inconclusive, conduct screening aortic imaging for biological relatives.
Conduct screening aortic imaging for first-degree relatives of patients with known history of TAD or pathogenic variants.
Record the aortic diameter in medical records for patients with acute type A aortic dissection to aid management of relatives.

content.Synopsis: A major risk factor for aortic root aneurysms, ascending aortic aneurysms, and aortic dissection is a pathogenic variant in genes predisposing to TAD. Genetic testing may play a role in many TAD patients. A multigene panel for HTAD is cost-effective. Clinical genetic testing is integral to evaluating patients with TAD showing indicators of single gene disorder.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: 1. 13% to 20% of patients with TAD and without Marfan/Loeys-Dietz/Vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome have affected first-degree relatives.
2. HTAD genetic testing panels include genes for systemic features overlapping with Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, or vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.
3. Specific genes have been associated with HTAD.
4. Genetic counseling should assist families in understanding TAD risk.

genes.context: FBN1 identified in individuals with HTAD who do not have Marfan syndrome symptoms.
TGFBR1 implicated in HTAD families lacking syndromic features.
TGFBR2 associated with risk for thoracic aortic aneurysm dissection, and related diseases.
SMAD3 identified in HTAD families without syndromic features.
TGFB2 mutation predisposes to multiple aneurysms and dissections.
ACTA2 mutations lead to TAA and occlusive vascular disease.
MYH11 confirmed in HTAD with significant aortic features.
MYLK confirmed in HTAD with aortic features.
LOX confirmed in HTAD families.
PRKG1 confirmed in families showing TAD features.

content.protocol: Evaluation and Genetic Testing for Patients with Thoracic Aortic Disease (TAD)
indications: Features of MFS, LDS, or vEDS
Family history of TAD, intracranial or peripheral aneurysm

testing.genetic_testing: Recommended for individuals with syndromic features, family history of TAD, and/or early age of disease onset.

positive_result.action: Cascade testing for at-risk relatives; gene-based management.

negative_or_vus_result.action: Imaging for aortic dilation/aneurysms in at-risk family members.

result_for_relatives.negative_test: Offspring at general population risk for TAAD.

content.recommendations: Thoracic aortic imaging is recommended for first-degree relatives of all individuals with TAD, regardless of age of onset, to detect asymptomatic aneurysms.

content.guideline: The guideline discusses the process of screening for individuals at risk of thoracic aortic enlargement due to inherited genetic variants, particularly those with pathogenic variants for heritable thoracic aortic disease (HTAD). It emphasizes the need for imaging for asymptomatic thoracic aortic dilation both in individuals with pathogenic variants and their relatives. The document also recommends screening and genetic counseling for family members of patients with the disease, especially when no genetic variant is identified. It suggests regular monitoring and tracking of aortic size for surgical intervention considerations.

genes.context: Pathogenic variants in genes for HTAD confer a high risk for TAD, so individuals found to have these pathogenic variants should be screened with aortic imaging for asymptomatic TAD.

content.Summary: Heritable thoracic aortic aneurysms and dissections (HTAD) can occur due to a highly penetrant rare variant or mutation in a single gene. Such conditions are familial, often involving genes related to external matrix proteins or transforming growth factor-β pathways. Syndromes like Marfan and Loeys-Dietz predispose individuals to rapid aortic growth and increased risk of dissection.
Clinical Recommendations: Screening for genetic conditions and family history due to higher risks involved in syndromic presentations.
Prophylactic surgery is often recommended to prevent aortic rupture and extend the lifespan of patients with HTAD.
The GenTAC study supports the utility of proactive monitoring and potential surgical interventions when appropriate.

genes.context: Pathogenic variants in genes related to extracellular matrix proteins are often implicated, particularly in relation to conditions like Marfan syndrome.
Mutations involving transforming growth factor-β pathways (TGF-β) are noted in syndromic forms like Loeys-Dietz syndrome.

content.Title: Diagnostic and Surveillance Aortic Imaging in Marfan Syndrome
Main Points: Initial diagnosis and surveillance imaging for Marfan syndrome recommend a TTE (transthoracic echocardiogram) to measure aortic root and ascending aorta diameters, with follow-up after 6 months and annually if stable.
If TTE does not adequately visualize the aortic root or ascending aorta, a CT or MRI is recommended.
In adults with Marfan syndrome, a CT or MRI of the thoracic aorta is recommended for further assessment after an initial TTE.
After aortic root replacement, surveillance imaging of the thoracic aorta by MRI or CT is recommended annually initially, then every other year if stable.
Surveillance imaging every 3 to 5 years is recommended for potential abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) after aortic root replacement.
content.Synopsis: Marfan syndrome is an autosomal dominant connective tissue disorder caused by pathogenic variants in the FBN1 gene. The syndrome affects multiple systems including skeletal, ocular, pulmonary, cutaneous, nervous, and cardiovascular. Imaging focuses on the aortic root, and surveillance for potential aortic aneurysms and dissections is critical due to risk factors including rapid growth and family history.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Aortic root dilation and aortic dissection are the leading causes of morbidity and mortality in Marfan syndrome.
Aortic dilatation involves the aortic root and can progress to include the sinotubular junction and ascending aorta.

genes.context: Marfan syndrome is an autosomal dominant connective tissue disorder caused by pathogenic variants in the FBN1 gene affecting 1 in 5,000 individuals.

content.title: 2022 ACC/AHA Aortic Disease Guideline — Excerpt: Marfan Syndrome
content.overview: Patients with Marfan syndrome may develop disease of the ascending and descending aorta. Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) can assess aortic root, ascending aorta, aortic arch, proximal and distal descending aorta; CT or MRI can be used when more distal segments are not adequately visualized. Surgical aortic root replacement can prevent type A aortic dissection and improve longevity for patients with Marfan syndrome and aortic root aneurysms. Long-term complications after aortic root replacement include graft infections, pseudoaneurysms, aneurysms in the distal aorta, and aortic dissection distal to the graft.

Medical_Therapy_Recommendations_for_Marf.Recommendation: In patients with Marfan syndrome, treatment with either a beta blocker or an ARB, in maximally tolerated doses (unless contraindicated), is recommended to reduce the rate of aortic dilation.
In patients with Marfan syndrome, the use of both a beta blocker and an ARB, in maximally tolerated doses (unless contraindicated), is reasonable to reduce the rate of aortic dilation.

content.Synopsis: Beta blockers have long been recommended to reduce heart rate, myocardial contractility, and slow aortic root growth in Marfan syndrome. ARBs have more recently been found to be efficacious in some studies.

Recommendation_Specific_Supportive_Text.1: Open-label study: Propranolol for >10 years was associated with reduction in aortic root growth rate and fewer clinical events compared with control (no treatment).
Recommendation_Specific_Supportive_Text.2: Retrospective evaluation in children with Marfan syndrome found beta-blocker treatment associated with reduced aortic growth rate.
Recommendation_Specific_Supportive_Text.3: Losartan prevented aneurysm formation in mouse models of Marfan syndrome.
Recommendation_Specific_Supportive_Text.4: Small nonrandomized open-label study of children with prior rapid aortic root growth suggested ARBs dramatically slowed aortic root growth.
Recommendation_Specific_Supportive_Text.5: Randomized trials comparing an ARB to a beta blocker in Marfan patients found no significant difference in aortic root growth rate or clinical events between the two treatments.
Recommendation_Specific_Supportive_Text.6: Multiple trials compared adding an ARB to beta-blocker therapy; in some studies addition of an ARB reduced aortic root growth over 3- to 5-year follow-up, and a meta-analysis confirmed slower growth rates with combination therapy.

Interventions_Replacement_of_Aortic_Root.Recommendation: In patients with Marfan syndrome and an aortic root diameter of ≥5.0 cm, surgery to replace the aortic root and ascending aorta is recommended.

In patients with Marfan syndrome, an aortic root diameter of ≥4.5 cm and features associated with an increased risk of aortic dissection, surgery to replace the aortic root and ascending aorta is reasonable when performed by experienced surgeons in a multidisciplinary aortic team.
Interventions_Replacement_of_Aortic_Root.notes: See referenced Table 10 for features associated with increased risk of aortic dissection.

additional_clinical_points: In some individuals thorough TTE may accurately assess diameters of aortic root, ascending aorta, aortic arch, and descending aorta; if segments distal to ascending aorta are not adequately visualized, use CT or MRI.

In Marfan syndrome, distal thoracic aortic aneurysm (TAA) and abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) may occur but are less common than root disease; many individuals with distal aortic disease had prior root replacement or smoke cigarettes.
Long-term complications after root replacement include graft infections, pseudoaneurysms, distal aneurysms, and distal aortic dissection.

content.summary: Prophylactic aortic root replacement prevents aortic dissection and improves survival in Marfan syndrome. The size threshold for elective surgery is based on factors such as age, height, family history, and rate of aortic growth. Aortic diameter and the risk of dissection increase with aortic size.

recommendations.point: 1. For patients with Marfan syndrome and a dilated aortic root, early elective aortic root replacement improves survival.
2. In at least 20% of patients with Marfan syndrome who have undergone early elective surgery for an aortic diameter <5.0 cm, outcomes are better.
3. Aortic growth rates vary; prophylactic aortic root replacement is suggested when the cross-sectional area to height ratio is ≥10 cm²/m.
4. Aortic root replacement with valve-sparing procedures offers favorable outcomes.

factors.description: The size and growth rate of the aorta are key factors influencing surgical decisions.
Aortic root replacement is associated with low surgical risk when done by experienced teams.

content.Marfan Syndrome Interventions: For patients with Marfan syndrome and a nondissected aneurysm of the aortic arch, descending thoracic aorta, or abdominal aorta of ≥5.0 cm, surgical intervention to replace the aneurysmal segment is reasonable.
content.Aneurysms: Marfan syndrome most commonly leads to aneurysms of the aortic root and ascending aorta but may affect the distal aorta and its branches.
content.Surgical Threshold: Using a 5.0-cm diameter threshold for surgery is reasonable.
content.Operative Intervention: In patients acceptable for operative repair, intervention is reasonable at an aortic diameter threshold of ≥5.0 cm.

Additional Context for Loeys-Dietz Syndr.Baseline Imaging: A baseline TTE, MRI, or CT is recommended to evaluate aortic dimensions and other arterial branches.
Additional Context for Loeys-Dietz Syndr.Surveillance: Regular imaging surveillance is recommended every 2 to 3 years depending on the condition and family history.

content.Synopsis: Loeys-Dietz syndrome is characterized by aortic and branch vessel aneurysms and dissections, arterial tortuosity, and unique craniofacial and cutaneous features. Imaging with CT or MRI, from head to pelvis, is recommended for evaluation.
content.Recommendation: In patients with Loeys-Dietz syndrome, treatment with a beta blocker or an ARB, or both, in maximally tolerated doses, is reasonable.

genes.context: Pathogenic variants in Loeys-Dietz syndrome genes, in particular TGFBR1 and TGFBR2, may have earlier onset TAD.
All the Loeys-Dietz syndrome genes confer a risk for aortic involvement distal to the aortic root along with branch vessel and intracranial aneurysms, with TGFBR1 and TGFBR2 pathogenic variants.
Pathogenic variants in SMAD3 are associated with premature osteoarthritis and later onset of TAD. Among patients with SMAD3-related disease, aneurysms from head to pelvis are also described.
There is much less information about the aortic and branch vessel disease in patients with variants in TGFB2 and TGFB3.
There is much less information about the aortic and branch vessel disease in patients with variants in TGFB2 and TGFB3.

content.Synopsis: The management of individuals with Loeys-Dietz syndrome includes medical therapy, lifestyle modification, imaging surveillance, and surgical intervention. To lessen hemodynamic stress on the aorta, beta blockers and ARBs have been used. No randomized trials of medications to reduce aortic growth or dissection risk.

Recommendations for Aorta Replacement.Recommendation: In patients with Loeys-Dietz syndrome and aortic dilation, surgical threshold for prophylactic aortic root and ascending aortic replacement should take into account specific genetic variant and other factors.
In patients with Loeys-Dietz syndrome attributable to pathogenic variants in specific genes, surgery may be considered based on several factors.

content.Additional Details: Surgery to prevent aneurysm disease in Loeys-Dietz syndrome is effective. Variants in specific genes may cause early aortic dissection. Loeys-Dietz syndrome also involves management of genetic, phenotypic, and risk factors.

genes.context: Aortic dissection in Loeys-Dietz syndrome is linked to pathogenic variants in TGFBR1 and other genes.
Patients with TGFBR2 variants may have earlier aortic dissection than those with Marfan syndrome.
SMAD3-related variant results in variable diameters leading to aortic dissection.
In limited data, TGFB2 variants indicate less aggressive phenotype.
TGFB3 variants are mentioned in the context of aortic dissection risk.

content.Information: The document provides guidelines for surgical thresholds for aortic root and ascending aortic replacement in Loeys-Dietz Syndrome based on genetic variants. It includes considerations for aortic surgery based on genetic variants, presence of high-risk features, and aortic diameter in centimeters. The text also discusses vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, which is associated with pathogenic variants in COL3A1 and results in aortic and arterial dissections, aneurysms, and rupture. Medical therapy includes lifestyle modifications and avoidance of invasive procedures. Surgical repair involves risk due to vascular fragility.

genes.context: TGFBR1 is mentioned in the context of surgical thresholds for prophylactic aortic root and ascending aortic replacement in Loeys-Dietz Syndrome. The aortic surgery may be recommended based on genetic variants like TGFBR1.
TGFBR2 is discussed in surgical guidelines for aortic surgery in Loeys-Dietz syndrome, considering the presence of high-risk features.
SMAD3 is referenced in relation to surgical thresholds for aortic replacement in Loeys-Dietz syndrome.
TGFΒ2 is mentioned within the discussion of surgical thresholds based on genetic variants.
TGFΒ3 is included in the list of genetic variants considered for surgical thresholds in aortic conditions.
COL3A1 is attributed to pathogenic variants leading to aortic and arterial dissections, aneurysms, and ruptures in vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.

content.title: 6.1.2.5. Turner Syndrome

diagnostic_recommendations.text: In patients with Turner syndrome, TTE and cardiac MRI are recommended at the time of diagnosis to evaluate for BAV, aortic root and ascending aortic dilation, aortic coarctation, and other congenital heart defects.

In patients with Turner syndrome who are ≥15 years old, the use of the ASI (ratio of aortic diameter [cm] to BSA [m²]) is recommended to define the degree of aortic dilation and assess the risk of aortic dissection.

In patients with Turner syndrome without risk factors for aortic dissection, surveillance imaging with TTE or MRI to evaluate the aorta is recommended every 5 years in children and every 10 years in adults, as well as before planning a pregnancy.
In patients with Turner syndrome and an ASI >2.3 cm/m², surveillance imaging of the aorta is recommended at least annually.
In patients with Turner syndrome and risk factors for aortic dissection, surveillance aortic imaging at an interval depending on the aortic diameter, ASI, and aortic growth rate is recommended.

In patients with Turner syndrome who are ≥15 years old and have an ASI of ≥2.5 cm/m² plus risk factors for aortic dissection, surgical intervention to replace the aortic root, ascending aorta, or both is reasonable.
In those without risk factors for aortic dissection, surgical intervention to replace the aortic root, ascending aorta, or both may be considered.

content.synopsis: Turner syndrome, affecting 1 in 2,500 liveborn girls, results from complete or partial loss of the second X chromosome. Approximately 50% have cardiovascular defects like BAV, aortic coarctation, and dilation. Patients are at increased risk for aortic dissection. Recommended evaluations include TTE, cardiac MRI, and calculating ASI.
recommendation_support: Turner syndrome diagnosis involves evaluation for congenital cardiovascular abnormalities using TTE and cardiac MRI.
Patients have short stature; thus, aortic diameters should be indexed to body size. ASI is calculated for patients aged ≥15 years old.

content.Title: Risk Factors for Aortic Dissection in Patients With Turner Syndrome
Details: Aortic coarctation, aortic dilation, bicuspid aortic valve, and hypertension are risk factors.
Aortic size index (ASI) >2.0 cm/m² is considered abnormal.
ASI ≥2.5 cm/m² is associated with increased risk of aortic dissection.
Turner syndrome-specific z-score is used for assessing children <15 years.
Surveillance imaging guidelines vary for children and adults based on risk factors.
Frequent surveillance imaging is needed when ASI approaches or exceeds thresholds.
Additional imaging is recommended before planned pregnancy.
content.Table: Risk factors and recommended surveillance protocols based on ASI and presence of other conditions (CoA, BAV, HTN).
content.Figure: Suggested aortic monitoring protocol for girls and women with Turner Syndrome aged ≥15 years.

content.summary: The guideline discusses surveillance recommendations and surgical thresholds for aortic root and ascending aortic replacement in patients with Turner syndrome and those with pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants in certain genes like ACTA2, PRKG1, MYH11, MYLK, and LOX. For Turner syndrome patients >15 years old with an ASI >2.3 cm/m², annual imaging of the aorta is appropriate. Decisions for surgery are based on retrospective studies, registries, and an individual's genetic variant, age, family history, and aortic measurements. Beta-blocker therapy is recommended in some instances to lessen hemodynamic stress.
content.specifics: Specific features for ACTA2, PRKG1, MYH11, MYLK, and LOX gene variants are discussed, including their impact on aortic diameters and potential for aortic dissection. Surgical recommendations vary by genetic variant and associated risk factors.

genes.context: Patients with ACTA2 mutations primarily present with type A or B aortic dissection, have aneurysms that involve the root and ascending aorta, and a subset of pathogenic variants predispose to occlusive vascular diseases. Surgical threshold for aortic diameter with ACTA2 mutation is set at ≥4.5 cm.

PRKG1-related HTAD can present in the late teens with type A or B aortic dissection without previous aortic enlargement. Surgical threshold for aortic diameter with PRKG1 mutation is set at ≥4.2 cm without risk factors and ≥2.0 cm with risk factors.
Patients with MYH11 mutations primarily present with type A or B aortic dissection and may have peripheral arterial disease. Surgical threshold guidance is provided.
Patients with MYLK mutations present at age >40 years with type A aortic dissection and little previous enlargement of the aorta.
Patients with LOX mutations can present with aortic root aneurysms, fusiform dilation of the aortic root, and may have mild systemic features of Marfan syndrome.

content.Synopsis: BAV is a common congenital valve condition, affecting about 1% of the population with a higher prevalence in males. It can occur sporadically or as an autosomal dominant trait with variable penetrance. BAV is often associated with aortic valve dysfunction and a risk of infective endocarditis. Patients may experience aortic dilation or aneurysms, primarily affecting the aortic root and ascending aorta. Surveillance for aortic aneurysms is crucial.
Recommendations: TTE is recommended for evaluating valve morphology and aortic dimensions in BAV patients.
CT or MRI is suggested when TTE cannot accurately assess the aortic structure.
Genetic evaluation is advised for BAV patients with HTAD or phenotypic concerns related to Loeys-Dietz syndrome.
Screening of first-degree relatives by TTE is recommended to evaluate the presence of BAV or aortic dilation.
content.Aortic Dilation: Primarily affects the aortic root and ascending aorta, with a prevalence between 20% to 84% depending on the definition and population studied.

Diagnostics.TTE: Used for evaluating function, dilation, and coarctation.
Diagnostics.CT/MRI: Used for superior imaging of aortic root and ascending aorta.
Diagnostics.Considerations: Method choice depends on personal characteristics and resource availability.

genes.context: Variants in TGFRB1 are associated with Loeys-Dietz syndrome.
Variants in TGFRB2 are associated with Loeys-Dietz syndrome.
HTAD attributable to pathogenic variants in NOTCH1 shows an increased prevalence of BAV.
HTAD attributable to pathogenic variants in ACTA2 shows an increased prevalence of BAV.
HTAD attributable to pathogenic variants in MYH11 shows an increased prevalence of BAV.
SMAD3 variants are associated with an increased prevalence of BAV.
LOX variants have an increased prevalence of BAV.

Clinical Information: Both BAV (Bicuspid Aortic Valve) and aortic root and ascending aortic dilation may be familial.
Inheritance patterns for familial BAV and aortopathy are consistent with an autosomal dominant pattern with incomplete penetrance.
In families with BAV and aortic root and ascending aortic dilation, obligate carriers may have BAV, aortic dilation, both, or neither.
Screening of first-degree relatives (parents, siblings, children) with TTE (Transthoracic Echocardiogram) is recommended to evaluate for BAV and aortic dilation.
CT or MRI is recommended if the ascending aorta is not adequately assessed by TTE.
Prevalence of a BAV in relatives of patients with BAV ranges from 9% to 20%.
TTE screening of first-degree relatives proves to be cost-effective.

Recommendations.Detail: Patients with a BAV who have undergone aortic valve repair/replacement and have a diameter of aortic root, ascending aortic, or both of ≥4.0 cm should have lifelong surveillance imaging of the aortic root and ascending aorta by TTE, CT, or MRI.
Patients with a BAV and a diameter of aortic root, ascending aortic, or both of ≥4.0 cm should have lifelong surveillance imaging of the aortic root and ascending aorta by TTE, CT, or MRI.

content.Title: BAV Aortopathy Interventions: Replacement of the Aorta in Patients With BAV

Key Points.Recommendation: In patients with a BAV and a diameter of the aortic root, ascending aorta, or both of ≥5.5 cm, surgery to replace the aortic root, ascending aorta, or both is recommended.
In patients with a BAV and cross-sectional aortic root or ascending aortic area (cm²) to height (m) ratio of ≥10 cm²/m, surgery to replace the aortic root, ascending aorta, or both is reasonable.
In patients with a BAV, a diameter of the aortic root or ascending aorta of 5.0 cm to 5.4 cm, and an additional risk factor for aortic dissection, surgery is reasonable.
In patients with a BAV undergoing surgical aortic valve repair or replacement with a diameter of the aortic root or ascending aorta of ≥4.5 cm, concomitant replacement may be reasonable.
In patients with a BAV, a diameter of the aortic root or ascending aorta of 5.0 cm to 5.4 cm, no other risk factors, and at low surgical risk, surgery may be reasonable.

content.Synopsis: The timing of surgery to replace the aorta in BAV disease depends on morphology, diameter of the aorta, aortic valve function, rate of aortic growth, family history, patient characteristics, patient wishes, and surgical expertise.

Recommendations for AAA Screening.Recommendation: In men who are ≥65 years of age who have ever smoked, ultrasound screening for detection of AAA is recommended.
In men or women who are ≥65 years of age and who are first-degree relatives of patients with AAA, ultrasound screening for detection of AAA is recommended.
In women who are ≥65 years of age who have ever smoked, ultrasound screening for detection of AAA is reasonable.
In men or women <65 years of age and who have multiple risk factors for AAA or a first-degree relative with AAA, ultrasound screening for AAA may be considered.
In asymptomatic men or women >75 years who have had a negative initial ultrasound screen, repeat screening for detection of AAA is not recommended.

content.Synopsis: AAA shares risk factors with typical atherosclerosis. AAA is characterized by medial degeneration of the aortic wall. AAA development is due to ongoing degradation stimulated by oxidative stress, smooth muscle cell apoptosis, proteolysis of the extracellular matrix, and adventitial inflammation. The hereditary and environmental risk factors include older age, male sex, smoking, and a positive family history.
content.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Older age, male sex, and smoking are independent strong risk factors for AAA. Smoking history impacts women greater than men, attributed to smoking variegates depending on use, with lowest risk in those who smoke fewer cigarettes than those with higher use.

content.Guideline Focus: The guideline provides an algorithm for identifying patients to screen for Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA) based on age, gender, smoking history, and family history.

Screening Recommendations.Criteria: Multiple risk factors or family history of AAA

Strong Risk Factors: Family history of abdominal aortic aneurysm

Statistics: Screening of men ≥65 years reduced long-term AAA-related mortality and AAA-related ruptures over 12 to 15 years.
Lifetime prevalence of AAA in brothers is estimated to be 32%.

sections.title: Practical implementation and outcomes of screening
sections.content: Screening in women remains uncertain and warrants further study.

sections.title: Risk factors and observations
sections.content: Patients under 65 may be at increased risk of AAA rupture. Most patients with AAA were under 65 years when ruptured, and data is lacking on the mortality benefit for screening. Patients over 75 may develop AAA even with a negative initial screen. Select patients at low surgical risk may need individualized screening based on measurements and risk factors.

sections.title: Growth and Natural History of Aortic Aneurysms
sections.content: Growth rates vary based on conditions like HTAD, Marfan syndrome, and others. Correlation exists with aortic dissection, rupture, and mortality. Patients with Marfan syndrome often see a growth rate of 0.26 cm/year for the aortic root. BAV patients have a growth rate around 0.06 cm/year.

sections.title: Medical Management of Sporadic and Degenerative Aortic Aneurysm Disease
sections.content: The goal is reducing growth rates, aortic-related mortality, and repair need while decreasing non-aortic cardiovascular events. Lifestyle modification, alongside pharmacotherapy such as beta blockers and ARBs, is recommended. Data from trials are limited outside Marfan and sporadic cases.

sections.title: Recommendations for BP Management in TAA
content: Use antihypertensive medications to reduce cardiovascular events in patients with elevated BP.
Beta blockers are suggested for achieving target BP goals.
ARB therapy is reasonable as an alternative to beta blockers.

content.Synopsis: The goal of BP control in TAA is to slow growth and prevent aortic dissection, as well as to reduce non-aortic cardiovascular events, such as myocardial infarction and stroke. Uncontrolled hypertension increases the risk for aortic dissection, so achieving a SBP goal of ≤130 mm Hg and a DBP goal of ≤80 mm Hg, with the use of antihypertensive therapy in those with hypertension and TAA, is advised.
content.Clinical Trials: Multiple trials have examined the effects of beta-blocker and ARB therapy on TAA. Beta-blockers have shown efficacy in slowing the rate of aortic root growth, especially in Marfan syndrome. ARBs have not demonstrated as significant an impact in comparison to beta-blockers.

Recommendations for Treatment of TAA Wit.2a C-LD: Statin therapy is recommended for patients with TAA and imaging or clinical evidence of atherosclerosis.
Recommendations for Treatment of TAA Wit.2b C-LD: Statin therapy may be considered for patients with TAA without evidence of atherosclerosis.

genes.context: Genetic aortopathies related to TAA are detailed in Section 6.1.2, 'Genetic Aortopathies'. Marfan syndrome, associated with mutations in FBN1, is specifically mentioned with regards to beta-blocker therapy effects.

content.Synopsis: Clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) encompasses aortic aneurysms of atherosclerotic origin. A high-intensity statin is recommended for patients <75 years with clinical ASCVD. Statins may benefit genetically mediated aneurysms if atherosclerotic disease is elsewhere. The pleiotropic effects of statins may reduce aortic growth. Smoking cessation is recommended for patients with thoracic aortic aneurysm (TAA).
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: 1. Atherosclerotic aortic aneurysms increase the risk of stroke and myocardial infarction.
2. The 2016 AHA/ACC guideline recommends high-intensity statins for noncoronary atherosclerotic disease to achieve an LDL goal of <70 mg/dL.
3. Statins may benefit vascular wall remodeling in TAAs.
4. A study showed that smoking cessation reduces risks for patients with TAA.

Antiplatelet Therapy in TAA.Recommendation: In patients with atherosclerotic TAA and concomitant aortic atheroma or PAU, the use of low-dose aspirin is reasonable, unless contraindicated.
Antiplatelet Therapy in TAA.Synopsis: Aortic aneurysms of atherosclerotic origin are considered a coronary artery disease equivalent with a >20% risk of an event within 10 years. Low-dose aspirin is recommended in patients with atherosclerotic aortic disease.
Antiplatelet Therapy in TAA.Important Study: The SPARC study associates aortic atherosclerosis with coronary artery disease, supporting aspirin use.

BP Management in AAA.Recommendation: In patients with AAA and an average SBP of ≥130 mm Hg, or an average DBP of ≥80 mm Hg, use of antihypertensive medication is recommended.
BP Management in AAA.Synopsis: Reducing cardiovascular events, such as myocardial infarction and stroke, are main goals in antihypertensive therapy for AAA. Achieving specific SBP and DBP goals can reduce adverse events.
BP Management in AAA.Considerations: Updated guidelines suggest a target SBP of <130 mm Hg and/or DBP <80 mm Hg for patients with clinical cardiovascular disease.

content.Synopsis: ASCVD includes noncoronary atherosclerotic disease such as peripheral artery disease (PAD) and AAA. AAA of atherosclerotic cause is considered a coronary artery disease equivalent. Intensive cholesterol lowering in patients with ASCVD reduces major cardiovascular events by an additional 15% beyond less intensive cholesterol lowering.

Recommendations.1: In patients with AAA and evidence of aortic atherosclerosis, statin therapy at moderate or high intensity is recommended.
Recommendations.2b: In patients with AAA but no evidence of atherosclerosis, statin therapy may be considered.

content.Additional Information: Statin use is associated with decreased 30-day mortality rates in patients with ruptured AAA. In patients undergoing AAA repair, those on statin therapy had improved survival. Statins have not been shown to slow growth rate of AAA or affect matrix metalloproteinase-9 or interleukin-6 concentrations.

Smoking Cessation in AAA.Recommendation: In patients with AAA who smoke cigarettes, smoking cessation efforts are recommended.
Smoking Cessation in AAA.Details: Cigarette smoking is a major risk factor for AAA development, growth, and complications. Strategies include smoking cessation and avoidance of secondhand smoke using the 5 A’s approach (Ask, Advise, Assess, Assist, Arrange).

Antithrombotic Therapy in AAA.Recommendation: In patients with AAA with concomitant atheroma and/or PAU, the use of low-dose aspirin may be considered, unless contraindicated.
Antithrombotic Therapy in AAA.Details: Aspirin, in doses of 75 mg to 162 mg daily, is recommended to reduce cardiovascular events and mortality. Antithrombotic therapy may benefit AAA patients but studies on antiplatelet efficacy are limited.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with a dilated thoracic aorta, a TTE is recommended at the time of diagnosis to assess aortic valve anatomy, aortic valve function, and thoracic aortic diameters.
In patients with a dilated thoracic aorta, a CT or MRI at the time of diagnosis is reasonable to assess thoracic aortic anatomy and diameters.

In patients with a dilated thoracic aorta, follow-up imaging (with TTE, CT, or MRI, as appropriate based on individual anatomy) in 6 to 12 months is reasonable to determine the rate of aortic enlargement; if stable, surveillance imaging every 6 to 24 months (depending on aortic diameter) is reasonable.

Details: A detailed baseline assessment of the segments of thoracic aorta, aortic valve anatomy, and aortic valve function is important.
TTE, CT, and MRI are common modalities used for assessment of the thoracic aorta.
Patients with stable aortic dimensions can be observed longitudinally with TTE, CT, or MRI.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with an AAA of 3.0 cm to 3.9 cm, surveillance ultrasound is recommended every 3 years to assess for interval change.
In men with an AAA of 4.0 cm to 4.9 cm and in women with an AAA of 4.0 cm to 4.4 cm, surveillance ultrasound is recommended annually to assess for interval change.
In men with an AAA of ≥5.0 cm and women with an AAA of ≥4.5 cm, surveillance ultrasound is recommended every 6 months to assess for interval change.

content.Synopsis: In patients with Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA), imaging assessment of the abdominal aorta is essential for establishing baseline diameter and determining the timing of surveillance imaging. Ultrasound is standard for surveillance and CT offers superior visualization, necessary for preoperative planning. MRI is a reasonable alternative to CT in selected cases.
Recommendation Specific Supportive Text: 1. Ultrasound surveillance of AAAs helps prevent rupture and mortality. The risk of rupture increases at diameters >5.5 cm for men and >5.0 cm for women. Surveillance should be more frequent for larger diameters and can be longer for diameters 3.0–3.9 cm.
2. In patients with AAA of 4.0-4.9 cm, annual surveillance ultrasound is recommended. Shorter intervals are often used for those who smoke, have diabetes, or both.

3. Once AAA size is ≥5.0 cm in men and ≥4.5 cm in women, screening every 6 months is suggested. CT provides superior visualization and MRA is an alternative. Non-IV contrast MRI is useful in defining AAAs.
4. CT is preferred when AAA reaches intervention thresholds, to confirm aortic diameters, and for preoperative planning.
content.Figure 20 Description: Shows the frequency of surveillance imaging of abdominal aortic aneurysms based on diameter: 3.0-3.9 cm imaging every 3 years, 4.0-4.9 cm imaging every 12 months, ≥5.0 cm imaging every 6 months for men and ≥4.5 cm for women.

content.section: 6.5 Surgical and Endovascular Management of Aortic Aneurysms
content.description: Most patients with thoracic aortic aneurysm (TAA) and abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) are asymptomatic. Surgical or endovascular intervention aims to reduce the risk of adverse aortic events like dissection, rupture, and death. The goal of open surgery is to replace the aneurysmal segment with a prosthetic graft. Endovascular repair utilizes stent grafts.
specific_notes: FDA-approved stent grafts are used for descending thoracic, juxtarenal, and infrarenal aortic segments.
Use of these grafts outside specified criteria may increase risk.
Careful selection for long-term surveillance is necessary.

surgical_recommendations.description: Surgery for symptomatic aneurysms of the aortic root and ascending aorta.
Surgery for asymptomatic patients with an aortic root or ascending aorta aneurysm diameter ≥5.5 cm.
Surgery for aortic root or ascending aorta aneurysm <5.5 cm with rapid growth rate.
Surgery for asymptomatic aneurysm diameter ≥5.0 cm when performed by specialized teams.
Replacement in patients undergoing valve surgery with associated ascending aorta aneurysm diameter ≥4.5 cm.
For aneurysms with specific morphology or growth rate under specialized care.
Asymptomatic patients with certain body size metrics qualifying for surgery.

content.Synopsis: Elective surgery for aneurysms of the aortic root and ascending aorta is recommended when the risk of adverse events, such as dissection, rupture, or death, outweighs the risks of surgery. Observational studies evaluate parameters such as aortic diameter and stress to predict adverse events. Aortic dissection or rupture can result from stress on the aneurysm wall. The primary criterion for elective surgery is based on diameter, under principles such as LaPlace’s law.

Recommendations.1: Large aneurysms may compress nearby structures causing symptoms or indicate rapid aortic growth, increasing the risk of aneurysm rupture.

Recommendations.2: A maximum aortic diameter of ≥5.5 cm has been the criterion for elective surgical repair, based on studies examining adverse event diameter thresholds. This applies regardless of patients having tricuspid or BAVs.

Recommendations.3: Studies have shown that ascending aortic aneurysm growth is slower than previously reported. Rapid growth is linked to rupture risk.

Recommendations.4: Risk of aortic dissection or rupture correlates with diameter. Aneurysms of ≥5.0 cm are particularly high risk.

Recommendations.5: For patients with aortic aneurysms and valve issues, surgery may include repair or replacement to reduce complications.

guidelines.aortic_root_replacement: Aortic root replacement should be individualized based on patient factors including type of aortic valve surgery, age, and comorbidities.
guidelines.repair_prophylaxis: Prophylactic aortic replacement at a diameter of 5.0 cm might be reasonable to provide a margin of safety against aortic dissection.
guidelines.study_data: Data from IRAD shows ~60% of acute Type A aortic dissection patients had diameters of <5.5 cm. Most patients with aneurysms <5.5 cm who were managed medically did not suffer aortic dissection or rupture.
guidelines.risk_assessment: Recommendations for prophylactic repair at diameters <5.5 cm are proposed but lack comprehensive study in multicenter trials.

operational_guidelines.ascending_aorta_aneurysm_surgery: In patients with isolated aneurysm to the ascending aorta meeting surgery criteria, resection and interposition graft replacement should be performed.
operational_guidelines.valve_repair_in_aneurysm: In patients undergoing aortic valve repair or replacement with an ascending aortic aneurysm, a separate aortic valve intervention is recommended.
operational_guidelines.root_replacement: For unsuitable aortic valves, mechanical or biological conduits are indicated.

content.Synopsis: The goal of prophylactic repair of aneurysms of the aortic root and ascending aorta is to prevent life-threatening complications like aortic dissection, rupture, or sudden death. This is best achieved when the surgery is scheduled electively rather than emergently, as indicated by the STS database showing a lower operative mortality rate for elective surgeries (2.2%) versus emergency surgeries (17.2%).
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Proximal thoracic aortic operations have an overall elective mortality rate of 2.2%. Patients meeting criteria for aneurysm repair with low operative risk can undergo prophylactic surgery with graft placement.
Adding ascending TAA repair to AVR does not increase operative mortality in experienced centers, but outcomes may vary in low-volume centers.
Root-sparing AVR is acceptable based on data showing the aortic root dilates slower than the ascending aorta.
Surgical approaches should consider the anatomy of the aortic valve, guiding whether a mechanical or biological-valved replacement is appropriate.
Elective valve-sparing aortic root replacement is safe in younger patients and those without underlying genetic disorders.
Aortic Arch Aneurysms: In symptomatic patients with low or intermediate risk, open surgical replacement is recommended.
In asymptomatic patients with isolated arch aneurysms and low risk, open surgical repair is reasonable at an arch diameter of ≥5.5 cm.
For ascending aortic aneurysms extending into the proximal aortic arch, consider extending the repair with a hemiarch replacement.
An elephant trunk procedure may be considered for aneurysmal disease extending to the proximal descending thoracic aorta.
Asymptomatic patients meeting intervention criteria with high risk from open surgery may be suited for hybrid or endovascular approaches.

content.Synopsis: Aortic arch aneurysms are the least common type of TAA, with most cases involving the arch only. Previous aortic dissection is the most common cause. There is a high risk associated with intervention procedures, but various strategies have been developed to manage this risk effectively.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Juxtaposition of the aortic arch to other structures can cause symptoms due to mass effect or compression. Ortner’s syndrome and dysphagia aortica are examples.
Open replacement of the aortic arch requires the use of bypass and other protective strategies.
A hemiaarch procedure might address distal pathology sufficiently when aneurysms extend to certain levels.
The elephant trunk procedure is useful for extensive TAD.
New hybrid and endovascular techniques have been developed to address higher-risk patients with aneurysms.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with intact descending TAA, repair is recommended when the diameter is ≥5.5 cm.
In patients with intact descending TAA and risk factors for rupture, repair may be considered at a diameter of <5.5 cm.
In patients at increased risk for perioperative morbidity and mortality, it may be reasonable to increase the size threshold for surgery accordingly.

content.Synopsis: The current aortic size threshold for repair of descending TAA is based on studies indicating that a diameter of >6 cm is associated with increased risk of adverse aortic events and mortality. Features such as rapid aortic growth, symptomatic aneurysms, underlying connective tissue disorders, or HTAD, and sacular aneurysm morphology are associated with a higher rupture risk. Advanced age, renal insufficiency, pulmonary disease, and previous stroke are markers for poor outcomes post repair.

genes.context: Features including rapid aortic growth, symptomatic aneurysms, underlying connective tissue disorder or HTAD, saccular aneurysm morphology, female sex, and infected aneurysm have all been associated with a higher tendency for rupture.

Definitions.Definite Event: Includes aortic dissection or rupture confirmed with imaging or intraoperative findings.
Definitions.Probable Event: Includes definite aortic events as well as sudden unexplained death.

High-Risk Features: Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz, or vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, or HTAD

Endovascular Repair: Zone 1/2 landing for thoracic stent graft

Endovascular vs Open Repair.Recommendation: In patients without Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, or vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, who have a descending TAA that meets criteria for intervention and anatomy suitable for endovascular repair, TEVAR is recommended over open surgery.
In patients with a descending TAA that meets criteria for repair with TEVAR, who have smaller or diseased access vessels, considerations for alternative vascular access are recommended.

In patients with a descending TAA that meets criteria for intervention, who have anatomy unsuitable for endovascular repair, and who are without significant comorbidities and have a life expectancy of at least 10 years, open surgical repair is reasonable.

genes.context: Marfan, Loeys-Dietz, or vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, or HTAD (see Section 6.1.2, 'Genetic Aortopathies')

content.Synopsis: TEVAR shows a reduced perioperative morbidity and aneurysm-related mortality compared to open surgical repair, but benefits diminish over time. In patients with connective tissue disorders or HTAD, or with longer life expectancy, open surgical repair is preferable. The choice between endovascular and open repair involves considering the need for anatomy and access, and the higher reintervention rate for TEVAR.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.TEVAR Benefits: Associated with reduced perioperative morbidity, reduced hospital length of stay, and better freedom from aneurysm-related mortality compared to open surgical repair. Short-term survival was better with TEVAR, but benefits decrease long-term.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Thoracic Endografting: Requires appropriate anatomy for delivery. Higher access complication rates, particularly in women.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Open Repair: Better outcomes in high-volume centers. Long-term survival is better with open repair for certain patient groups.

Recommendations.Description: In patients with descending TAA who undergo TEVAR with planned left subclavian artery coverage, revascularization of the left subclavian artery before TEVAR is recommended to prevent spinal cord injury (SCI) and potentially to reduce stroke risk and prevent other ischemic complications.

In patients with descending TAA who have undergone TEVAR with left subclavian coverage and develop SCI that is unresponsive to an increase in BP or a cerebrospinal fluid drain, left subclavian artery revascularization may be considered.

Left Subclavian Artery Management.Synopsis: Left subclavian artery coverage is required in up to 40% of cases of TEVAR of descending TAAs. SCI and stroke remain devastating complications associated with TEVAR. Preoperative left subclavian revascularization decreases the rates of stroke and SCI. Special considerations include vertebrobasilar insufficiency and left arm ischemia. Longer-term follow-up of this technology is needed, but initial results are promising. Trials are ongoing for branched endografts.

Recommendation.Description: In patients with descending TAA undergoing TEVAR in whom celiac artery coverage is being considered, it is reasonable to first confirm adequate collateralization.

Celiac Artery Management.Synopsis: Celiac artery coverage is necessary in 15% of patients undergoing TEVAR for descending TAA repair. Despite preoperative evaluation with CTA or angiography, a small percentage die from visceral ischemia.

content.Synopsis: Ruptured TAA carries a high mortality rate. Several studies and trials have shown lower rates of perioperative death and complications with endovascular repair compared to open surgical repair. However, advantages such as lower composite rates of death, stroke, and permanent paraplegia with TEVAR are not evident long term.

Recommendations.Details: In patients with ruptured descending TAA who are anatomic candidates for endovascular repair, TEVAR is recommended over open repair due to decreased perioperative death and morbidity.
In patients undergoing TEVAR, intentional coverage of the left subclavian artery, celiac artery, or both may be considered to increase the landing zone for endovascular repair.

Recommendations-Specific Supportive Text: Migration of the endograft distally over time can cause stenosis of the SMA and decrease flow to the SMA and celiac artery-based collaterals.
For repair of ruptured descending TAA, TEVAR is associated with decreased perioperative morbidity and mortality compared with open repair.

content.Section: 6.5.3.6. Access Issues for TEVAR in Descending TAA

1.Text: In patients with descending TAA undergoing TEVAR, review of preoperative CTA of the iliofemoral vessels should be performed to evaluate access.

2.Text: In patients with descending TAA undergoing TEVAR, if iliac access is marginal or inadequate to prevent access-related complications, the use of alternative conduits is recommended.

3.Text: In patients with descending TAA undergoing TEVAR who have suitable anatomy, total percutaneous femoral access is a reasonable alternative to open surgical cutdown to avoid access-related complications.

Synopsis.Text: Iliac artery access for stent-graft delivery systems is marginal in up to 21% of cases in which TEVAR is performed for descending TAA.
