Review of the CTA of the iliofemoral system is essential to ensure that marginal or inadequate access is managed. Alternative access was used in 9.4% to 21.1% of patients during clinical trials due to small or diseased access vessels.

Supportive Text: Thoracic endovascular stent grafts require a thorough review of the iliofemoral system to avoid access complications.
Alternative access was used in 9.4% to 21.1% of patients due to small or diseased access vessels.
Alternative access required in clinical trials for 21.1% of patients undergoing TEVAR.
Percutaneous access associated with fewer complications and shorter length of stay.

6.5.4.1.Title: Size Thresholds for Open Surgical Repair of TAAA

1.Text: In patients with intact degenerative TAAA, repair is recommended when the diameter is ≥6.0 cm.

2.Text: In patients with intact degenerative TAAA, repair is reasonable when the diameter is ≥5.5 cm and the repair is performed by experienced surgeons in a Multidisciplinary Aortic Team.

3.Text: In patients with intact degenerative TAAA who have features associated with an increased risk of rupture, repair is reasonable when the diameter is <5.5 cm.

features_risk_TAAA_rupture: Rapid growth (confirmed increase in diameter of ≥0.5 cm/y)
Saccular aneurysm or presence of penetrating atherosclerotic ulcers
summary.synopsis: The data supporting aortic diameter thresholds for TAAA repair are similar to those for descending TAA. Single-institution series with longitudinal follow-up show that intervention at diameters <6.0 cm can reduce aortic-related events and death. Intervention may be justified at smaller diameters in the presence of other risk factors. Aortic-related adverse events and death risk increase when TAAA diameters are >6.0 cm.

ruptured_TAAA: Endovascular repair may be reasonable for hemodynamically stable patients in centers with expertise and access to appropriate stent grafts.
intact_TAAA: Open repair is recommended for patients with Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, or vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.
Endovascular repair may be considered for patients with suitable anatomy and access to appropriate grafts.

genes.context: Intact TAAA: In patients with Marfan syndrome, open repair is recommended over endovascular repair.
Intact TAAA: In patients with Loeys-Dietz syndrome, open repair is recommended over endovascular repair.
Intact TAAA: In patients with vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, open repair is recommended over endovascular repair.

Synopsis.RCTs: There are no RCTs comparing early or late outcomes for open versus endovascular repair for TAAA.
Synopsis.Devices: As of November 2022, there are no FDA-approved devices for endovascular TAAA repair.
Synopsis.Procedures: Most endovascular repairs are performed with customized fenestrated or branched endografts under exemption or industry-sponsored trials.
Synopsis.Outcomes: Endovascular repairs show steady increase with limited follow-up.

Results.Open Repair: Operative mortality rate of 7.5% in TAAA patients.

Recommendation 1.Ruptured TAAA: Open repair can be performed with low mortality with specific protocol.
Recommendation 1.Registry Data: High rate of stroke and reintervention reported.

Recommendation 2.Endovascular Approach: Better for stable patients with suitable anatomy.
Recommendation 2.Kolbel Study: 15% mortality for symptomatic, 30% for ruptured TAAA.

Recommendation 3.Connective Tissue Disorders: Open repair recommended due to higher complications like stroke and SCI.

Recommendation 4.Outcome: High technical success in experienced hands, significant long-term survival rates.

content.title: Recommendations for TAAA Spinal Cord Protection

key_recommendations.text: In patients undergoing open TAAA repair who are at high risk for SCI, cerebrospinal fluid drainage is recommended to reduce the incidence of temporary SCI, permanent SCI, or both.

In patients who experience delayed spinal cord dysfunction after either open or endovascular TAAA repair, timely measures to optimize spinal cord perfusion and decrease intrathecal pressure are recommended.

content.synopsis: SCI is a complication of open and endovascular thoracoabdominal aneurysm repair with an incidence rate of 2% to 15%. The use of cerebrospinal fluid drainage is recommended to reduce the risk. Delayed SCI may occur up to 2 weeks after surgery, potentially affecting long-term outcomes.
management_techniques: Increase mean arterial pressure to >100 mm Hg

TAAA Renal and Visceral Organ Protection.recommendation: In patients undergoing open repair of TAAA involving the renal arteries, cold blood or crystalloid renal perfusion is recommended to provide effective protection against renal injury.

In patients undergoing open or endovascular TAAA repair who have end-organ ischemia or significant stenoses from atherosclerotic visceral or renal artery disease, additional revascularization procedures are recommended.

content.Synopsis: Postoperative renal dysfunction after open TAAA repair has a significantly negative impact on short- and long-term mortality as well as quality of life. Efforts to reduce renal injury include local organ hypothermia with active cold crystalloid or cold blood-based perfusate.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.point: Renal dysfunction after TAAA repair is defined as a doubling of the creatinine or the need for hemodialysis. Short- and long-term survival is compromised, and the incidence of postoperative respiratory failure, SCI, and cardiac complications increase.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.RCT_results: Two RCTs were performed comparing cold crystalloid renal preservation to normothermic blood perfusate and cold blood perfusate. Cold crystalloid infusion led to a threefold reduction in postoperative renal dysfunction.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.point: In patients with renal or visceral artery stenoses or ostial obstruction secondary to chronic or acute dissection flaps, end-organ perfusion may be compromised. Improvement in perfusion can be achieved by bypass, endarterectomy, or balloon angioplasty and stent placement.

AAA Access Recommendations.recommendation: In patients undergoing endovascular repair of AAA who have suitable common femoral artery anatomy, ultrasound-guided percutaneous access and closure is recommended over open cutdown. This reduces operative time, blood loss, length of stay, time to wound healing, and pain.

content.Synopsis: Increased availability of percutaneous closure devices and low-profile endovascular stent grafts have made ultrasound-guided percutaneous access and closure more feasible. Favorable outcomes from percutaneous common femoral artery access and closure include reduced operative time, reduced blood loss, and improved patient-centered outcomes like reduced pain.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: The PEVAR trial showed the noninferiority of total percutaneous access and closure for EVAR for those with suitable common femoral artery anatomy.
In the PIERÓ study, ultrasound-guided percutaneous access reduced the risk of surgical site infections compared with cutdown.
A multicenter observational study showed a significant decrease in groin hematomas with routine ultrasound-guided access.
Recommendations for Repair of Ruptured A: In patients with ruptured AAA who are hemodynamically stable, CT imaging is recommended to evaluate endovascular repair feasibility.
Endovascular repair is recommended over open repair to reduce morbidity and mortality for those with suitable anatomy.
Local anesthesia is preferred to reduce the risk of perioperative mortality in endovascular repair.
Permissive hypotension can be beneficial to decrease bleeding.
content.Mortality Rate: The mortality rate for rAAA is estimated to be 80% to 90%. For those presenting at a hospital, the historical mortality rate for open repair was approximately 50%. Improved treatment strategies have reduced this to as low as 18.5% in some cases.

content.Focus: Evaluating rAAA with endovascular vs open repair
Key Findings: No increased risk of death from acquiring preoperative imaging
rEVAR had superior survival rates compared to open repair
Substudies showed lower risk of mortality with local anesthesia in rAAA patients
Observations: Many authors support rEVAR in anatomically suitable candidates
Endovascular approaches show survival benefit in rAAA
Permissive hypotension suggested for managing rAAA
Recommendations: Use of CTA to assess suitability for endovascular or open repair
Consider endovascular devices with caution due to perioperative risk
Maintain low arterial pressures in rAAA to reduce bleeding risk

content.Threshold for AAA Repair: Patients with unruptured AAA should consider repair if the aneurysm diameter is ≥5.5 cm in men or ≥5.0 cm in women.
content.Symptoms: Repair is recommended for patients with symptoms attributable to the aneurysm to reduce risk of rupture.
content.Saccular AAA: Intervention for unruptured saccular AAA to reduce risk of rupture may be reasonable.
content.Aneurysm Growth: Patients with AAA growth ≥0.5 cm in 6 months should consider repair.
content.Risk Factors: Largest risk factor for rupture is maximum diameter. Risk increases with aneurysm size.
content.Clinical Trials: UKSAT and ADAM trials did not find a survival benefit for early aneurysm repair. Threshold of ≥5.5 cm is acceptable for men with infra-renal AAA.

content.clinical_guideline: The guideline discusses the treatment options for abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) with a focus on open and endovascular repair methods. It emphasizes the importance of a shared decision-making process for patients with nonruptured AAA with low to moderate operative risk. Recommendations include adherence to manufacturer’s instructions for elective endovascular repair and considering endovascular repair to reduce the risk of perioperative complications for patients with high perioperative risk. The guideline notes that sacular aneurysms are more common in women and more prone to be symptomatic at smaller sizes than fusiform aneurysms. Rapidly growing aneurysms may warrant consideration for repair.

Recommendations: Pooled data from 7 RCTs indicate lower risk of perioperative mortality with EVAR compared to open repair at 6 months. However, after 8 years, there's a higher risk of aneurysm-related and secondary intervention-related death with EVAR.
Patient-specific anatomical characteristics of aorta must be considered for EVAR. Deviating from manufacturer’s instructions increases failure rates.
EVAR-2 trial showed EVAR did not initially improve survival compared to no intervention but reduced aneurysm-related mortality over time.
Recent studies comparing open versus endovascular repair show similar mortality rates for complex aortic aneurysms.

content.guideline_section: 6.5.5.5 Treatment of Concomitant Common Iliac Aneurysms

recommendations.recommendation: For patients with asymptomatic small AAA and concomitant common iliac artery aneurysm(s) ≥3.5 cm, elective repair of both abdominal and iliac aneurysms is recommended.

When treating common iliac artery aneurysms or ectasia as part of AAA repair, preservation of at least 1 hypogastric artery is recommended, if anatomically feasible, to decrease the risk of pelvic ischemia.

content.synopsis: Iliac artery aneurysms are common in patients with both aortic and iliac aneurysms, with prevalence reported up to 40%. Repair considerations include avoiding hypogastric artery exclusion to prevent ischemic complications like buttock claudication and erectile dysfunction.
supportive_text: A case series showed common iliac artery aneurysms of an average rate of 2.9 mm/yr.
In a meta-analysis, exclusion or preservation of the internal iliac artery correlated with increased buttock claudication occurrences.

recommendations.Recommendations: Baseline surveillance imaging with CT is recommended at 1 month postoperatively for patients with AAA treated with EVAR. If there is no evidence of endoleak or sac enlargement, continued surveillance with duplex ultrasound at 12 months and then annually thereafter is recommended.

For patients with AAA treated with EVAR undergoing annual surveillance imaging with abnormal findings, additional cross-sectional imaging with CT or MRI of the abdomen and pelvis every 5 years postoperatively is reasonable.
For patients with AAA treated with complex EVAR, a modified surveillance imaging plan that combines cross-sectional imaging and duplex ultrasound of target vessels is reasonable.
For patients with AAA who have undergone open repair, surveillance imaging with CT or MRI of the abdominopelvic aorta within 1 year postoperatively and then every 5 years thereafter is reasonable.

content.synopsis: Routine surveillance after EVAR aims to detect endoleak, sac growth, endograft migration, or failure. CT is the gold standard but expensive and involves ionizing radiation. Duplex ultrasound can detect endoleaks but has limited accuracy for migration or noncontiguous aneurysms. MRI offers high spatial accuracy but requires radiographs for stent fractures. Surveillance after open AAA repair prevents new aneurysms and aneurysm-related death. Patients with AAA and iliac arteries disease are at risk of aortic aneurysm in noncontiguous areas.
supportiveText: The incidence of late aortic rupture after EVAR is >5% in 8 years of follow-up.
Endoleaks may occur in 10% to 17% of EVAR cases at 30 days postoperatively.
Duplex ultrasound is specific for endoleaks but has limitations in detecting stent migration.

content.Duplex ultrasound: Used for targeting branch vessel surveillance after FEVAR. Helpful in routine cross-sectional imaging for surveillance of fenestration sites, branch junctions, and flow adequacy in certain arteries.
content.Para-anastomotic aneurysms: These occur after open AAA repair with incidence rates at different postoperative years: 1%, 6%, 27% to 35% at 5, 10, and 15 years. Late aortic aneurysms found in different aortic repair segments with rising incidence 7 years postoperatively.
content.Surveillance recommendation: Vascular Surgery and European Society of Cardiology recommend imaging every 5 years post-AAA repair.

Acute Aortic Syndromes (AAS).Characteristics: Life-threatening, high mortality rate (1% to 2%/h). Symptoms include sharp or stabbing pain, mainly in chest or back, radiating at times.
Acute Aortic Syndromes (AAS).Significance: Presentation overlaps with other conditions. Requires prompt identification for proper therapy.
Acute Aortic Syndromes (AAS).Blood Pressure Measurement: Measure in both arms and lower extremities to detect differentials. Listen for murmurs to indicate conditions like aortic dissection.

Signs and symptoms of AAS.Cause: Incomplete aortic valve closure caused by tethering to aortic dilated area or cusp prolapse.
Spinal malperfusion due to intercostal artery involvement.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with a suspected AAS, CT is recommended for initial diagnostic imaging, given its wide availability, accuracy, and speed, as well as the extent of anatomic detail it provides.
In patients with a suspected AAS, TEE and MRI are reasonable alternatives for initial diagnostic imaging.

content.Synopsis: A plain chest x-ray is not sufficiently sensitive or specific for AAS diagnosis but certain radiographic findings may raise suspicion of aortic dissection. CT, TEE, and MRI are accurate for AAS diagnosis. Aortography is rarely used. Detection risk scores like AAD-RS or AORTAcan help evaluate patients presenting with AAS.

Imaging Modalities.CT: Shows full extent of dissection, detects aortic branch vessel involvement, vessel patency, signs of malperfusion, and other related conditions.
Imaging Modalities.TEE and MRI: Reasonable alternatives to CT but less commonly used as the first option.
Imaging Modalities.Aortography: Rarely used due to being more invasive and less sensitive.

content.Biomarkers: A non-elevated D-dimer (<500 ng/mL) may exclude AAS in low probability cases. Low D-dimer might be useful in diagnosis exclusion.

Aortic Simplified Score (AORTAS) Pretest.Scoring: The patient is given the number of points corresponding to each clinical item that is positive in the patient's presentation. The points are summed, and a total score of 0 to 1 point is low-probability of aortic dissection, where a total of 62 points is high probability.

Recommendations for Acute Medical Manage.Recommendation: In patients presenting to the hospital with AAS, prompt treatment with anti-impulse therapy with invasive monitoring of BP with an arterial line in an ICU setting is recommended as initial treatment to decrease aortic wall stress.
Patients with AAS should be treated to an SBP < 120 mm Hg or to lowest BP that maintains adequate end-organ perfusion, as well as to a target heart rate of 60 to 80 bpm.
In patients with AAS, initial management should include intravenous beta blockers, except in patients with contraindications.
In those with contraindications or intolerance to beta blockers, initial management with an intravenous non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker is reasonable for heart rate control.
In patients with AAS, initial management should include intravenous vasodilators if the BP is not well controlled after initiation of intravenous beta-blocker therapy.
Patients with AAS should be treated with pain control, as needed, to help with hemodynamic management.

content.Synopsis: Patients presenting with AAS need to be treated promptly to prevent acute and chronic complications. Immediate medical therapy is indicated while considering urgent surgical or endovascular intervention. Medical therapy plays an important role in decreasing long-term aorta-related adverse events.

content.intravenous vasodilators: Commonly used in initial treatment of patients with acute aortic syndrome (AAS) to decrease aortic wall stress.
content.beta blockers: Long-term beta blockers are recommended for patients with AAS unless contraindicated.
content.ACEIs and ARBs: Shown to be beneficial in the long-term management of hypertension in patients with aortic dissection.
content.calcium channel blockers: Use with caution, especially in patients with Marfan syndrome and other inherited aortopathy due to potential adverse effects.
content.pain management: Intravenous opiates are effective; NSAIDs like ketorolac should be avoided due to hypertension risk.

genes.context: Mouse models of Marfan syndrome as well as case control studies in Marfan syndrome indicate deleterious effects of long-term calcium channel blocker use.

Management Options: Immediate ascending aortic/arch surgery for rupture tamponade and stroke in Type A aortic dissection.
Endovascular fenestration or ascending aortic/arch surgery and antegrade TEVAR for mesenteric or renal malperfusion in Type A aortic dissection.
Target vessel stenting for ischemia persistence in Type A aortic dissection.
TEVAR or extra-anatomic bypass for Type B aortic dissection malperfusions such as rupture tamponade, mesenteric or renal malperfusion, and lower extremity ischemia.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with suspected or confirmed acute type A aortic dissection, emergency surgical consultation and evaluation and immediate surgical intervention is recommended due to high risk of associated life-threatening complications.
In patients with acute type A aortic dissection, who are stable enough for transfer, transfer from a low- to a high-volume aortic center is reasonable to improve survival.

In patients presenting with nonhemorrhagic stroke complicating acute type A aortic dissection, surgical intervention is reasonable over medical therapy to reduce mortality and improve neurologic outcomes.

content.Synopsis: Acute type A aortic dissection is a life-threatening condition. Suspected or diagnosed cases require urgent evaluation and surgical intervention to manage risk of adverse events like rupture, myocardial ischemia, or end-organ malperfusion. From 1995 to 2013, surgical mortality rate for acute type A decreased from 25% to 18%, but medical mortality rate stayed at 57%. Surgical intervention reduces immediate risk, corrects issues like AR, and can reverse malperfused vessels. Age impacts outcomes with surgical vs. medical management in elderly showing better outcomes. Consideration of risks vs. benefits is required, especially in patients with additional health concerns.
content.Recommendations: 1. Immediate evaluation and surgical intervention are recommended to prevent adverse events (myocardial infarction, AR, cardiac tamponade, aortic rupture, end-organ malperfusion). Mortality rate for untreated cases is high and increases with time delay in surgical intervention. Timeliness of intervention is critical, with particular attention to neurological outcomes.

Recommendations for Management.1: For patients with acute type A aortic dissection presenting with renal, mesenteric, or lower extremity malperfusion, immediate operative repair of the ascending aorta is recommended.
Recommendations for Management.2: For patients with acute type A aortic dissection with clinically significant mesenteric malperfusion, immediate operative repair of the ascending aorta or mesenteric revascularization is reasonable.

Synopsis.Prevalence: Malperfusion is present in as many as 25% of patients with acute type A aortic dissection.
Synopsis.Mortality: Malperfusion syndrome has a mortality rate of 30.5% compared to 6.2% for those without.
Synopsis.Management: Cardiac and visceral malperfusion show poor outcomes. Interventions to reestablish perfusion should be based on specific conditions and available resources.

Conditions.Cardiac: Electrocardiographic changes, troponin elevation, myocardial dysfunction
Conditions.Mesenteric: Abdominal pain, bowel ischemia, lactic acidosis
Conditions.Extremity: Loss of pulses, sensory or motor dysfunction

content.guideline: Surgical Repair Strategies in Acute Type A Aortic Dissection

recommendations.recommendation: Patients with acute type A aortic dissection and a partially dissected aortic root but no significant aortic valve leaflet pathology should undergo aortic valve resuspension rather than valve replacement.

Patients with acute type A aortic dissection with extensive destruction of the aortic root, a root aneurysm, or a known genetic aortic disorder should undergo aortic root replacement with a mechanical or biological valved conduit.
For stable patients, valve-sparing root repair may be considered when performed by experienced surgeons in a Multidisciplinary Aortic Team.
In patients with acute type A aortic dissection undergoing aortic repair, an open distal anastomosis is recommended to improve survival and increase false-lumen thrombosis rates.
Patients with acute type A aortic dissection without an intimal tear in the arch or a significant arch aneurysm should undergo hemiarch repair rather than more extensive arch replacement.

For aortic dissection with a dissection flap extending through the arch into the descending thoracic aorta, extended aortic repair with antegrade stenting of the proximal descending thoracic aorta may be considered to treat malperfusion and reduce distal aortic complications.

perfusion_and_cannulation.strategy: Axillary cannulation for patients undergoing surgical repair is reasonable to reduce risk of stroke or retrograde malperfusion, compared to femoral cannulation.
For patients who require circulatory arrest, cerebral perfusion is reasonable to improve neurologic outcomes.
Direct aortic or innominate artery cannulation with imaging guidance is reasonable as an alternative to femoral or axillary cannulation.

content.synopsis: To reduce late aortic complications, surgical resection should include the tear site, any aneurysmal aorta, and proximal-most extent of the dissection. Risk of future aortic dilation, insufficiency, or repeat dissection is addressed through a more extensive replacement involving the aortic root, arch, or both.

genes.context: Patients with acute type A aortic dissection with extensive destruction of the aortic root, a root aneurysm, or a known genetic aortic disorder should undergo aortic root replacement with a mechanical or biological valved conduit.

clinicalGuidelines.Finding: No difference in perioperative mortality or survival when comparing root replacement with more limited root repair or supracommissural replacement.
Standardized structured algorithmic approach showed a mortality rate of only 8.1% with aortic valve resuspension as preferred approach.
Reoperation rates are mixed for freedom after reoperation, but 2 meta-analyses have shown excellent long-term durability of aortic valve resuspension.
clinicalGuidelines.Reoperation Rates: 1.4% to 2.1% per patient-year and low thromboembolism and bleeding rates (1.4%/patient-year).

clinicalGuidelines.Finding: Aneurysmal root diameter >4.5 cm is a risk factor for late reintervention.
Involvement of the aortic arch by the aortic dissection can influence intervention strategies and clinical outcomes.
Total arch replacement (TAR) is safe and promotes aortic remodeling.

clinicalGuidelines.Meta-Analysis: Lower mortality rates with partial compared with TAR.

clinicalGuidelines.Observational Data: Higher rates of excessive bleeding and thromboembolic complications without appropriate neurological deficits in hemiaarch versus TAR group.

content.summary: The guideline discusses management strategies for acute type A and type B aortic dissection. Antegrade cerebral perfusion is associated with improved neurologic outcomes and lower long-term mortality rates. Axillary cannulation carries risks such as higher stroke rates. Several studies emphasize the importance of medical therapy and consider endovascular interventions under specific conditions.
content.typeA_management: Cerebral perfusion techniques (antegrade, bilateral) are highlighted for improved outcomes in acute type A aortic dissection. Seldinger technique for rapid cardiopulmonary bypass setup is recommended.

typeB_management.recommendation: For uncomplicated cases, medical therapy is recommended as the initial strategy.
Intervention is recommended when there are complications or rupture, with open or endovascular repair considered based on anatomy.
Endovascular management can be considered for uncomplicated cases with high-risk anatomic features.

content.research: Introduced endovascular techniques improve morbidity and mortality rates. Randomized trials (ADSORB, INSTEAD) support these findings.

Features.Comment: This can be either free or contained (including hemothorax, increasing periaortic hematoma, or both; or mediastinal hematoma) and should be addressed promptly.
Complete or partial occlusion of a major branch, with or without clinical evidence of ischemia; this includes visceral, renal, and peripheral arterial branches.
Extension of the dissection flap either distally or proximally (ie, retrograde type A dissection)
Progressive enlargement of the true, false, or both lumens while in the acute phase may require prompt intervention.

Patients Presentation.Details: Patients with complicated acute type B aortic dissection or who develop such features after initial presentation have an increased risk of morbidity and death, and urgent or emergency intervention may be required.

Importance of Evacuative and Endovascula.Details: Rapid coverage of the affected region of the descending aorta can be lifesaving and does not preclude subsequent further endovascular or open repair.

References: TEVAR showed lower mortality rates compared to open repair, benefiting older patients.
The VIRTUE Registry showed reintervention rates of 20% to 39%

Medical Management.Concerns: Uncomplicated type B aortic dissection has a 30-day mortality rate of 10% and decreased long-term survival; interest in endovascular intervention to reduce downstream complications.

genes.context: Patients with genetically triggered aortic diseases (eg, Marfan syndrome) have an increased risk when presenting with complicated type B aortic dissection.
Patients with genetically triggered aortic diseases (eg, Loeys-Dietz syndrome) have an increased risk when presenting with complicated type B aortic dissection.

content.Synopsis: Aortic IMH is a distinct pathologic entity from aortic dissection and PAU. It's characterized by hemorrhage within the media of the aortic wall and may occur with or without intimal disruption. Radiographically, it appears as a high-attenuation crescent or circumferential thickening of the aorta. IMH is more common in the descending thoracic aorta (60%) than in the ascending aorta (30%) or aortic arch (10%). Symptoms are similar to aortic dissection, but patients tend to be older with hypertension and atherosclerosis. IMH can progress to aortic enlargement, dissection, or rupture. Prevalence varies by region, with 6% to 23% in North America/Europe, and 26% to 44% in Asia.

Management Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with complicated acute type A or type B aortic IMH, urgent repair is recommended.
In patients with uncomplicated acute type A IMH, prompt open surgical repair is recommended.
In selected patients with uncomplicated acute type A IMH with high-risk features, medical management may be considered.
In patients with uncomplicated acute type B IMH, medical therapy is recommended as the initial management strategy.
In patients with type B IMH requiring distal aortic arch repair with favorable anatomy, endovascular repair is reasonable.
In patients with type B IMH requiring distal aortic arch repair with unfavorable anatomy, surgical repair is reasonable.
In patients with uncomplicated type B IMH and high-risk features, intervention may be reasonable.

Key Points: IMH, especially type A IMH, is a serious condition that can lead to complications like rupture.
A nonoperative strategy for type A IMH is linked to high mortality, while surgical repair offers better outcomes.
Type B IMH generally has a better prognosis than type A, with lower mortality rates when managed medically or surgically if needed.
Different strategies and timing for surgery are discussed, including 'timely' surgical intervention based on individual patient risk factors.
Management Strategies: For type A IMH, surgery is often necessary, with strategies varying based on patient condition and associated risks.
In type B IMH, medical management is preferred unless symptoms worsen, requiring surgical intervention.
Endovascular treatment options like TEVAR (Thoracic Endovascular Aortic Repair) are considered, particularly for type B IMH or complicated cases.
Considerations: Mortality and outcomes significantly depend on the timing of intervention, patient risk factors, and hospital expertise.
The approach may vary by region and population due to genetic or environmental factors influencing disease progression.

Type A IMH: Focal intimal disruption with ulcer-like projection involving ascending aorta or arch
Type B IMH: Focal intimal disruption with ulcer-like projection involving descending thoracic aorta if developed in acute phase

PAU With IMH, Rupture, or Both.Recommendation: In patients with PAU of the aorta with rupture, urgent repair is recommended.
In patients with PAU of the ascending aorta with associated IMH, urgent repair is recommended.
In patients with PAU of the aortic arch or descending thoracic aorta with associated IMH, urgent repair is reasonable.
In patients with PAU of the abdominal aorta with associated IMH, urgent repair may be considered.

content.Synopsis: A Penetrating Aortic Ulcer (PAU) is an atherosclerotic lesion of the aorta with ulceration that can penetrate the elastic lamina of the aortic wall, potentially progressing to Intramural Hematoma (IMH), aortic dissection, or rupture. PAUs with IMH are associated with a high risk of disease progression, particularly when localized to the ascending aorta. Data on outcomes for PAU combined with descending thoracic and abdominal aorta are limited but show significant early disease progression among patients treated with medical management.

Recommendations.Text: In patients with isolated PAU who are symptomatic and have persistent pain that is clinically correlated with the radiologic findings, repair is recommended.
In patients with isolated PAU who are asymptomatic but have high-risk imaging features, elective repair may be considered.

content.Summary: For patients with severe atherosclerotic disease, weighing the risk of repair against patient life expectancy and risk of progression is crucial. PAUs are increasingly diagnosed incidentally and have been shown to have radiographic progression in a portion of patients.

High-Risk Imaging Features of PAUs: Significant growth of PAU diameter or depth
PAU associated with a saccular aneurysm
PAU with an increasing pleural effusion

Symptomatic PAUs.Risk: High risk of early disease progression
Symptomatic PAUs.Progression Rate: 30% had disease progression on surveillance imaging at a mean of 18 months follow-up
Symptomatic PAUs.Complications: Expansion of the PAU and new IMH in 20% and conversion to aortic dissection in 10%
Symptomatic PAUs.Treatment: Most patients treated with open or endovascular repair

Asymptomatic PAUs.Risk: Associated with disease progression if they have large diameter or depth, significant growth on surveillance imaging, or other high-risk features
Asymptomatic PAUs.Non-High-Risk Progression: Low risk of progression (3.6% and 6.5% at 5 and 10 years respectively)

Recommendations for PAU Open Surgical Re.1: In patients who require repair of a PAU in the ascending aorta or proximal aortic arch (zones 0-1), open surgical repair is recommended.
Recommendations for PAU Open Surgical Re.2a: In patients who require repair of a PAU in the distal aortic arch (zones 2-3), descending thoracic aorta, or abdominal aorta, either open surgical repair or endovascular repair is reasonable, based on anatomy and medical comorbidities.

content.Synopsis: Operative repair of PAUs includes both open and endovascular treatment. Historically, most PAUs were treated with open aortic replacement, with good outcomes for descending and infrarenal aorta repairs. Available data is based on small studies with a high risk of treatment bias. Midterm outcomes after endovascular repair show a 4% to 8% risk of endoleak and a small risk of new PAU formation. Comparative data is limited for ascending aortic PAUs. Despite this, open repair is a standard for treating AAS in the ascending aorta and proximal arch.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.1: Open surgical repair results for the ascending aorta and proximal arch can be applied to PAU. There is no FDA-approved device for endovascular repair in these areas.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.2: The risk of procedure-related and in-hospital death is lower for endovascular repair in descending thoracic and abdominal aorta.

Recommendations for Initial Management o.1: For patients with BTTAI, management at a trauma center with expertise is recommended.
Recommendations for Initial Management o.2: For patients with BTTAI, anti-impulse therapy is advised to reduce injury extension and rupture risk, except for those with hypotension or hypovolemic shock.

content.Summary: Blunt Thoracic Aortic Injury (BTTAI) is a serious condition often resulting from high-speed deceleration impacts and is the second-leading cause of trauma-related death. Improved imaging techniques have increased diagnosis rates. Immediate care following polytrauma is crucial, with a focus on managing blood pressure and heart rate to prevent rupture.
content.Diagnosis and Mortality: Diagnosis of BTTAI increased significantly from 2003 to 2013. Mortality rate is approximately 19% for patients treated in the emergency room. Early repair showed increased odds of death. Nonoperative management is recommended, especially for low-grade injuries.

Clinical Guidelines.Society for Vascular Surgery: Suggests urgent repair if serious co-injuries exist. However, optimal timing remains unclear. Delayed repair may show better survival rates.
Clinical Guidelines.Multidisciplinary Care: Emphasizes level 1 trauma center care for concomitant polytrauma management.
Clinical Guidelines.Medical Management: Beta blockers are suggested in the acute setting to reduce aortic wall stress.

Recommendations.Recommendation: Nonoperative management with follow-up imaging for patients with grade 1 BTTAI.
Aortic intervention recommended for patients with grade 3 to 4 BTTAI and nonprohibitive comorbidities.
Aortic intervention for patients with grade 2 BTTAI and high-risk imaging features.
Nonoperative management with follow-up imaging for patients with grade 2 BTTAI without high-risk features.

content.Aorta Involvement: Most commonly involves the aortic isthmus. Proximal ascending (8%-27%), aortic arch (8%-18%), and distal descending segments (11%-21%) may also be involved.

Management Recommendations: Nonoperative vs operative management depends on factors like patient stability and injury characteristics.
Grade 1 injuries are managed medically and associated with low mortality.
Grades 3 and 4 are at high risk of progression and require urgent treatment.
Nonoperative management for grade 3 was an independent predictor of all-cause death.
Grades 1 and 2 had similar outcomes whether managed nonoperatively or by TEVAR.
High-Risk Imaging Features of BTTAI: Lesion to normal aortic diameter ratio >1.5:4
Ascending aortic arch or great vessel involvement

content.recommendation: In patients with BTTAI who meet indications for repair and with appropriate anatomy, TEVAR is recommended over open repair.
content.synopsis: Endovascular therapy for BTTAI has become the predominant approach. From 2007 to 2015, rates of open repair decreased while rates of TEVAR increased. No randomized trials for open versus endovascular management have been conducted. TEVAR offers superior 30-day mortality rates and lower rates of SCI and acute kidney injury.
content.recommendation_specific_supportive_text: Endovascular treatment of BTTAI is associated with improved procedural and 30-day mortality rates, as well as postoperative complications. TEVAR was associated with lower procedural and 30-day mortality rates in meta-analysis.
recommendations_for_BAAI: In patients with grade 1 to 2 BAAI without malperfusion, anti-impulse therapy is recommended along with repeat imaging.
In patients with grade 4 BAAI, repair should be done to address life-threatening aortic injury.
In patients with grade 2 BAAI and associated malperfusion, consider repair.
For BAAI, endovascular or open repair is reasonable and depends on various factors.
In patients with grade 3 BAAI, it may be reasonable to consider repair.
Usage of REBOA for hemorrhage control is unclear and may cause harm.

content.Synopsis: BAAI represents a rare traumatic entity, occurring in less than 1% of patients with blunt trauma. Patients with BAAI often have concomitant injuries such as rib fractures, abdominal visceral injury, and cardiac complications that will affect treatment decisions. BAAI injuries are graded based on aortic contour defects, and this grading is used to assess the risk of major morbidity and death from injuries. Patients with Grade 1 aortic injuries are generally managed with antihypertensive therapy, beta blockade, and antiplatelet therapy, with repeat scan at 24 to 48 hours. Grade 2 injuries are similarly managed nonoperatively. Grade 3 injuries may need endovascular treatments. Grade 4 injuries may require rapid control of hemorrhage through surgery. The use of REBOA for hemorrhage below the diaphragm is discussed but has mixed guidance. Zones of injury for surgical approaches are outlined.
content.Recommendation: Data aggregated from 12 trauma centers are presented, noting that most patients with Grade 1 and 2 injuries were managed conservatively.

Blunt Aortic Injury Grades.Description: Minor intimal tear, intimal defect, or thrombus (≤10 mm in length or width)
Large intimal flap, intimal defect, or thrombus (>10 mm in length or width)

Management.Nonoperative: Some injuries may not show progression and do not require intervention.
Management.Operative: Required for patients with progression, symptoms, or grade 4 injuries.
Management.Endovascular vs Open Repair: Endovascular is preferred for certain injuries; no FDA-approved devices for the abdominal aorta.

Specific Findings.Grade 4 Injuries: High mortality rate; many cases in the first 24 hours due to cardiac arrest.
Specific Findings.Grade 2 Injuries: Can be managed nonoperatively unless progression occurs.

REBOA.Use: Emerging for hemorrhage control, but associated with increased risk in certain scenarios.
REBOA.Contraindications: Pericardial tamponade and major thoracic hemorrhage.

content.Outcomes: Mortality and risk differ based on injury grade and management choice.

Recommendations for Long-Term Management.Recommendation: In patients with BTAI who have undergone aortic repair, surveillance imaging at intervals appropriate for the repair approach and location is reasonable.

In patients with BTAI who have not undergone repair, surveillance imaging with a CT at 1 month, 6 months, and 12 months after the diagnosis and, if stable, at appropriate intervals thereafter may be reasonable.

content.Synopsis: In-hospital and midterm outcomes after open and endovascular repair for BTAI are favorable for patients who survive to hospital discharge. However, long-term data are limited. The SVS guidelines suggest that follow-up after TEVAR could be decreased to every 2 to 5 years in the absence of abnormalities. No published guidelines are available for postoperative surveillance after open or endovascular abdominal aortic repair for blunt aortic injury. Long-term data on blunt aortic injuries managed nonoperatively are also limited.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Endograft malposition and endoleak may occur in some patients immediately after endovascular repair, but midterm data suggest low incidence of complications.
Injury progression occurred in 7.6% of nonoperatively managed patients, and improvement was observed in 34% of patients after a range of 1 day to 118 months.

Long-Term Surveillance Imaging After Aor.Recommendation: In patients with acute aortic dissection and IMH treated with either open or endovascular repair and have residual aortic disease, surveillance imaging with a CT (or MRI) is recommended after 1 month, 6 months, and 12 months and then, if stable, annually thereafter.

In patients with acute aortic dissection and IMH that was managed with medical therapy alone, surveillance imaging with a CT or MRI is recommended after 1 month, 6 months, and 12 months and then, if stable, annually thereafter.

content.Synopsis 1: Survival after an acute aortic dissection and intramural hematoma (IMH) doesn't ensure freedom from subsequent aortic events due to residual aortic dissection and risk of aneurysm formation. Ten-year survival post-repair of acute type A aortic dissection is 60% to 65%. Risk of reoperation is higher for the aortic valve, aortic root, and distal aorta, with an aortic root reoperation rate of about 15% in 15 years. The distal aorta grows about 1 mm/year, with reoperation rates ranging from 10% to 16% at 10 years. Despite TEVAR providing protection from early aortic-related death in type B aortic dissection, reintervention after TEVAR ranges from 27% to 39% at midterm follow-up. Surveillance imaging post-thoracic aneurysm repair is essential for monitoring disease progression and reintervention needs.
content.Recommendation 1: Patients with uncomplicated type B aortic dissection have a favorable prognosis. Regular surveillance imaging is essential to detect disease progression in diseased and non-diseased aorta. Using TTE and cross-sectional imaging helps in monitoring aortic anatomy and valve function over time.
content.Recommendation 2: For patients with previous acute aortic dissection and IMH, regardless of initial treatment, elective thoracic aortic repair is advised for those with chronic residual TAD and aneurysm with a total aortic diameter of ≥5.5 cm.
content.Recommendation 3: Reoperation after type A aortic dissection repair has low complication rates, primarily for aneurysms of the thoracic aorta, aortic anastomotic pseudoaneurysms, progressive aortic regurgitation, or graft infection. Operative mortality for elective repair is less than 10%. False-lumen thrombosis occurs in 62% of extent 3B dissection and 91% of extent 3A dissection after TEVAR, with reintervention rates at TEVAR ranging from 15% to 26% at 5 years.
content.Recommendation 4: For patients with a PAU post-repair, it's reasonable to have surveillance imaging at appropriate intervals based on repair approach and location. If managing medically, a CT is recommended at 1 month post-diagnosis, then every 6 months for 2 years, and at appropriate intervals thereafter.

Synopsis.clinical_failure: For patients who undergo repair of a PAU, clinical failure (defined as endoleak, disease progression, graft occlusion, repeat aortic intervention, or procedure or aortic-related death) by 1 year after endovascular and open repair occur in 8.6% and 8.7%, respectively.
Synopsis.disease_risk: For patients with PAUs who are managed non-operatively, the risk of disease progression is significant. Disease progression occurs more frequently in patients presenting with symptomatic versus asymptomatic PAUs but is >15% for both. Among patients with a PAU who have progression of disease on surveillance imaging, 73% will show continued worsening on subsequent imaging, and 46% will have progression to frank dissection after a mean of 12 months.

content.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: After open or endovascular repair of a PAU, there is a 9% risk of clinical failure by 12 months postoperatively.
content.Freedom_from_complications: Freedom from cumulative complications and interventions is 86% at 12 months, 79% at 24 months, and 71% at 36 months postoperatively.
content.Series: In a series of 109 patients with acute PAUs, 28% suffered from an aortic-related adverse event by 30 days of follow-up.
content.Systematic Review: Based on a systematic review of 184 patients with either thoracic or aortic PAU, 30% had radiographic evidence of disease progression on midterm follow-up. For patients with abdominal PAU, 23% have disease progression on CTA by 8 months of follow-up, although the risk is higher in symptomatic (43%) versus asymptomatic (17%) patients.

Pregnancy.counseling_and_management: Counseling and management of aortic disease in pregnancy and postpartum.
recommendations: In patients with genetic aortopathies attributable to syndromic (Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome) and nsHTAD and who are contemplating pregnancy, genetic counseling before pregnancy to discuss the heritable nature of their condition is recommended.
In patients with syndromic and nsHTAD, Turner syndrome, BAV with aortic dilation, and other aortopathy conditions, aortic imaging before pregnancy is recommended to determine aortic diameters.

In patients with syndromic and nsHTAD, Turner syndrome, BAV with aortic dilation, and other aortopathy conditions, who are contemplating pregnancy, counseling about the risks of aortic dissection related to pregnancy is recommended.
during_pregnancy: In patients with aortic aneurysms or at increased risk of aortic dissection, it is recommended that pregnancy be managed by a multidisciplinary team, and delivery be planned in a hospital where the capability for emergency aortic repair is available.
In patients with aortopathies who are pregnant, guideline-directed treatment of hypertension is recommended.
In patients with syndromic and nsHTAD, beta-blocker therapy during pregnancy and postpartum is recommended unless contraindicated.

genes.context: In patients with genetic aortopathies attributable to syndromic (Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome) and nsHTAD and who are contemplating pregnancy, genetic counseling before pregnancy to discuss the heritable nature of their condition is recommended.

In patients with genetic aortopathies attributable to syndromic (Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome) and nsHTAD and who are contemplating pregnancy, genetic counseling before pregnancy to discuss the heritable nature of their condition is recommended.

In patients with genetic aortopathies attributable to syndromic (Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome) and nsHTAD and who are contemplating pregnancy, genetic counseling before pregnancy to discuss the heritable nature of their condition is recommended.

content.summary: The guideline discusses risks and management of aortic diseases during pregnancy, specifically in women with aortopathy. It emphasizes pre-pregnancy counseling, genetic testing, and regular imaging to monitor aortic diameters. Aortic dissection can occur without significant dilation, particularly in women with certain genetic conditions. There is a focus on individualized patient care based on genetic considerations.
content.pregnancy_risks: Aortic dissection is a risk during pregnancy, particularly in the third trimester or postpartum. Vaginal delivery is preferred unless complications necessitate cesarean.

conditions.notes: Includes conditions like Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Turner syndrome.

content.diagnostic_methods: TTE, MRI, or CT imaging pre- and postpartum to monitor aortic size and function, avoiding gadolinium during pregnancy.
content.genetic_counseling: Recommended for women with aortopathy to evaluate hereditary risks and discuss genetic testing.

genes.context: In women with TGFRB1 or TGFRB2 pathogenic variants, aortic dissection was reported in 0% to 19% of pregnancies. Aortic growth in pregnancy is reported in Loeys-Dietz syndrome.
Limited data are available on pregnancy and SMAD3, TGFB2, or TGFβ3 pathogenic variants.

content.For women with aortopathic conditions, m: Beta-blocker therapy has shown benefits in Marfan syndrome to lessen aortic stress. ARBs and ACEIs are contraindicated during pregnancy due to teratogenicity risks. Calcium channel blockers are generally avoided in Marfan syndrome.

genes.context: Beta-blocker therapy has been shown to lessen aortic growth rates in Marfan syndrome.

content.clinical_summary: The risk of type A aortic dissection related to pregnancy in Marfan syndrome is associated with aortic root diameter, with low risk at diameters <4.0 cm, and higher risk at >4.5 cm. Type A and B dissections may occur in Marfan syndrome; type B can occur without significant dilation after aortic repair. Pregnancy should be avoided if the diameter is >4.5 cm. Elective cesarean delivery is often performed in women with Marfan syndrome and significant aortic dilation.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with Marfan syndrome and an aortic root diameter of >4.5 cm, aortic surgery before pregnancy is recommended.
If the aortic root diameter is 4.0 cm to 4.5 cm, aortic surgery before pregnancy may be considered.
In patients with Loeys-Dietz syndrome with pathogenic variants in TGFBR2 or TGFBR3 and an aortic diameter of ≥4.5 cm, surgery before pregnancy is reasonable.
If Loeys-Dietz syndrome is attributable to pathogenic variants in TGFBR1, TGFBR2, or SMAD3, and the aortic diameter is ≥4.0 cm, surgery before pregnancy may be considered.

genes.context: In patients with Loeys-Dietz syndrome attributable to pathogenic variants in TGFBR2 or TGFBR3, surgery before pregnancy is reasonable for aortic diameter of ≥4.5 cm.
In patients with Loeys-Dietz syndrome with variants in TGFBR2 or TGFBR3, surgery before pregnancy is reasonable for aortic diameter of ≥4.5 cm.
If Loeys-Dietz syndrome is attributable to variants in TGFBR1, TGFBR2, or SMAD3, surgery before pregnancy may be considered if the aortic diameter is ≥4.0 cm.
If Loeys-Dietz syndrome is due to variants in TGFBR1, TGFBR2, or SMAD3, surgery before pregnancy may be considered if the aortic diameter is ≥4.0 cm.

content.Synopsis: The decision to proceed with operative intervention for an aortic root, ascending aortic aneurysm, or both in a woman contemplating pregnancy is complex and depends on many factors. Considerations include specific disorder, genetic variant, rate of aortic growth, family history, and phenotype.
content.Decisions: Specialists involved in this decision may include aortopathic specialists, cardiologists, medical geneticists, maternal fetal medicine specialists, and aortic surgeons.
content.Risks: Risks include aortic surgery, pregnancy-related dissection distal to the aortic graft in HTAD.
Condition-specific notes: Women with Marfan syndrome and aortic root dilation >40 mm and especially >4.5 cm are at increased risk of type A aortic dissection.
Information is lacking about aortic diameters and aortic dissection risk related to pregnancy in Loeys-Dietz syndrome.
Patients with nsHTAD may manifest disease as acute aortic dissection.

genes.context: The risk may be higher in women with Loeys-Dietz syndrome attributable to pathogenic variants in TGFRB1 and TGFRB2.
Patients with TGFBR2- and TGFBR3-related Loeys-Dietz syndrome may have a lower aortic dissection risk.
Aortic dissection risk in those with variants in TGFRB1, TGFRB2, or SMAD3.
In patients with ACTA2 pathogenic variants, 20% of aortic dissections were related to pregnancy.
nsHTAD because of pathogenic variants in multiple genes (eg, ACTA2, MYH11, MYLK, PRKG1, and others).
nsHTAD because of pathogenic variants in multiple genes (eg, ACTA2, MYH11, MYLK, PRKG1, and others).
nsHTAD because of pathogenic variants in multiple genes (eg, ACTA2, MYH11, MYLK, PRKG1, and others).

content.title: Pregnancy in Patients With Aortopathy: Aortic Dissection and Aortic Surgery in Pregnancy

guidelines.recommendation: In patients experiencing an acute type A aortic dissection during the first or second trimester of pregnancy, urgent aortic surgery with fetal monitoring is recommended.
In patients experiencing an acute type A aortic dissection during the third trimester of pregnancy, urgent cesarean delivery immediately followed by aortic surgery is recommended.

notes: Shared decision-making is required to determine the surgical threshold before elective aortic root, ascending aortic surgery, or both.
Aortic dissection related to pregnancy has occurred at small aortic diameters in women with ACTA2 and MYH11 pathogenic variants.

Surgery before pregnancy at smaller aortic diameters is informed by aortic growth rate, hypertension, surgical expertise, patient wishes, and other factors involving a shared decision depending on individual circumstances.

genes.context: Loeys-Dietz syndrome (attributable to pathogenic variants in TGFBR1, TGFBR2, or SMAD3)
Loeys-Dietz syndrome (attributable to pathogenic variants in TGFBR1, TGFBR2, or SMAD3)
Loeys-Dietz syndrome (attributable to pathogenic variants in TGFBR2 or TGFBR3)
Loeys-Dietz syndrome (attributable to pathogenic variants in TGFBR1, TGFBR2, or SMAD3)
Aortic dissection related to pregnancy has occurred at small aortic diameters in women with ACTA2 and MYH11 pathogenic variants.
Aortic dissection related to pregnancy has occurred at small aortic diameters in women with ACTA2 and MYH11 pathogenic variants.

clinicalGuidelines.text: In patients experiencing an acute type B aortic dissection during pregnancy, medical therapy is recommended, unless endovascular or surgical therapy is required to manage acute complications.
In patients with progressive aortic dilation during pregnancy, prophylactic aortic surgery may be considered, depending on individual circumstances.

In patients with large vessel vasculitis (LVV), prompt evaluation of the entire aorta and branch vessels, with MRI or CT, with or without 18F-FDG positron emission tomography (FDG-PET), is recommended.

content.summary: The guidelines address management of aortic conditions during pregnancy. It emphasizes individualized decision-making based on maternal and fetal risks. Prophylactic surgery for marked aortic dilation or rapid expansion is considered. In acute instances, early pregnancy aortic dissection may necessitate termination, while later instances require surgical or medical intervention. Inflammatory aortitis conditions like Takayasu Arteritis and Giant Cell Arteritis are highlighted for particular diagnostic approaches.

content.title: 2022 ACC/AHA Aortic Disease Guideline (excerpt) - Large Vessel Vasculitis (LVV)

recommendations.text: In patients with active GCA or Takayasu arteritis, initial medical therapy should include high-dose glucocorticoids.
In patients with GCA who have evidence of active aortitis, tocilizumab is recommended as adjunctive therapy to glucocorticoids, with methotrexate as an alternative.
In all patients with Takayasu arteritis, nonbiological disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs (DMARDs) should be given in combination with glucocorticoids.

In patients with active GCA or Takayasu arteritis, treatment efficacy should be periodically assessed by monitoring inflammatory serum markers (C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate), imaging with CT, MRI, or FDG-PET, and clinical symptoms.
In patients with GCA or Takayasu arteritis who are in remission, elective endovascular or open surgical intervention is reasonable to treat aortic and branch vessel complications.
In patients with GCA or Takayasu arteritis and aortic involvement who are in remission, annual surveillance imaging with CT, MRI, or FDG-PET is reasonable.

synopsis.overview: Large-vessel vasculitis (LVV) comprises Takayasu arteritis and giant cell arteritis (GCA), the most common causes of aortitis. Other causes include immunoglobulin G4-related disease, ANCA-associated vasculitis, sarcoidosis, Behçet's disease, relapsing polychondritis, and granulomatosis with polyangiitis; many cases remain idiopathic.
synopsis.anatomic_patterns: Takayasu arteritis and GCA tend to affect the thoracic aorta; immunoglobulin G4-related disease more commonly affects the abdominal aorta.
synopsis.complications: Potential complications include aortic aneurysms, aortic dissection, intramural hematoma (IMH), penetrating atherosclerotic ulcer (PAU), rupture, progressive atherosclerosis, and thrombotic complications.
synopsis.diagnostic_imaging_and_biomarkers: 18F-FDG-PET with CT is useful for diagnosis and to evaluate anti-inflammatory treatment response before planned revascularization. Sensitivity of diagnostic imaging decreases with duration of glucocorticoid treatment.
synopsis.initial_treatment_options: High-dose glucocorticoid therapy (prednisone 40-60 mg/day or equivalent) is standard induction therapy. For select cases, intravenous pulse steroids plus adjunctive therapy (including tocilizumab and methotrexate) may be used. Revascularization may be warranted in select stable cases.

recommendation_specific_supportive_text: Early imaging can confirm the diagnosis of suspected GCA or Takayasu arteritis and should be performed promptly to avoid treatment delays given the risk of complications from untreated LVV.

FDG-PET has reported high specificity for GCA-related aortitis (reported as high as 100%); CTA specificity is reported about 85%. CT findings such as wall thickening may be mistaken for atherosclerosis; CT is useful for assessing occlusive lesions, intimal injury, ulcerative plaques, and aneurysmal disease and is often combined with FDG-PET.
Evidence for MRI in GCA is limited, but MRI is widely used in Takayasu arteritis due to younger patient age at diagnosis and need for lifelong surveillance.
If proximal aortic involvement is confirmed by CT or MRI, echocardiography may help assess aortic valve function.
Active vasculitis is diagnosed by clinical symptoms of GCA or Takayasu arteritis with evidence of inflammation by serum biomarkers, imaging, or both.
High-dose glucocorticoid therapy (prednisone 40-60 mg/day or equivalent) remains standard induction therapy for GCA and Takayasu arteritis.
therapies_mentioned: Tocilizumab (adjunctive therapy for GCA with active aortitis)
diagnostic_modalities_mentioned: Serum inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate)
content.references_in_excerpt: Multiple guideline references cited (e.g., 1,2,7-21,22-26) as shown in the page excerpt.

Diagnosis Criteria: Systolic BP variation of >10 mm Hg between arms
Arteriographic evidence of aorta or aortic branch stenosis
Temporal artery tenderness or pulse attenuation

Giant cell arteritis (GCA): Use prednisone taper combined with tocilizumab
Interleukin-6 receptor inhibitor recommended for patients with refractory or relapsing disease
Adjuvant therapy with glucocorticoids individualized based on imaging and clinical findings
Takayasu arteritis: Initiate DMARDs with glucocorticoids for high relapse rates
Biological DMARDs as second-line agents for patients with relapse
Treatment duration not well understood; individualized approach recommended

Emphasis on: Surveillance imaging for new symptoms or signs

Evidence: High-quality randomized clinical trial evidence supporting the use of adjunctive therapy in Takayasu arteritis is limited

genes.context: An interleukin-6 receptor inhibitor, was superior to either a 26-week or 52-week prednisone taper plus placebo in reducing the primary outcome of glucocorticoid-free disease remission at 1 year.

content.title: 2018 EULAR Recommended Algorithm for the Pharmacological Treatment of Giant Cell Arteritis

initial_treatment.optional_treatment: Consider use of intravenous methylprednisolone 0.25–1 g/d for 3 d

phases.tapering: Taper GCs to 15–20 mg/d within 2–3 months and then to ≤5 mg/d after 1 year

no_target_achieved.minor_relapse: Increase GC to last effective dose

treatment.tapering: Taper GC (to 0 mg at 6 months with Tocilizumab)

content.Infectious aortitis: Infection of the aorta often caused by bacterial or fungal pathogens, leading to complications like aneurysm, erosion, fistula, dissection, or rupture. Medical imaging and open surgical repair are commonly recommended, though endovascular repair may be considered in select cases.
Management strategies: Intravenous antimicrobial therapy for at least 6 weeks

content.Management: Comparison of resection and extra-anatomic reconstruction, in situ reconstruction, or endovascular device repair for aortic mycotic aneurysm.

Procedures.Name: Extra-anatomic reconstruction
Procedures.Potential Indications: Infrarenal location with gross purulence, psoas or retroperitoneal abscess, vertebral osteomyelitis, inadequate response to antibiotic therapy, selected aortoenteric fistulae.
Procedures.Advantages: Avoids placement of foreign body in infected area.
Procedures.Disadvantages: Not technically feasible for thoracic, suprarenal, or visceral location or for emergency use, long operating time, long-term patency issues.

Procedures.Name: In situ reconstruction
Procedures.Potential Indications: Thoracic, suprarenal, infrarenal, or visceral location, selected aortoenteric fistulae.
Procedures.Advantages: More versatile than extra-anatomic, fewer long-term complications, higher patency rates, lower recurrent infection rate, shorter operating time.
Procedures.Disadvantages: Theoretical risk of infection from interposition of foreign material in infected sites, potential for ischemic colitis.

Procedures.Name: Endovascular device repair
Procedures.Potential Indications: Bridge procedure: hemodynamic instability, uncontrolled bleeding, rupture or impending rupture, selected patients with aortoenteric fistulae, patients not fit for open surgery.
Procedures.Advantages: Emergency stabilization, low early morbidity, mortality, less invasive.
Procedures.Disadvantages: Persistent infections and device infections, higher long-term morbidity, mortality, with device retention, requires device explanation, reconstruction.

content.General Clinical Information: The guideline discusses managing infectious aortitis or mycotic aneurysms with antimicrobial therapy and compares different surgical methods. A focus on reducing infection-related complications through open surgical repair or endovascular repair is highlighted. Prognosis remains poor for infectious aortitis and rupture. Appropriate treatment is decided based on anatomic factors, presence of fistulae, and clinical status. Expertise plays a key role in therapy direction.

content.Title: Diagnosis and Management of Prosthetic Aortic Graft Infection
content.Summary: The guideline provides recommendations for antimicrobial therapy and surgical options for patients with prosthetic aortic graft infections, with an emphasis on diagnosis, treatment, and late management strategies.
Diagnosis: For patients with a prosthetic aortic graft showing signs of unexplained infection or gastrointestinal bleeding, cross-sectional imaging is advised.
Treatment: Hemodynamically stable patients with infected grafts should consider open surgery with reconstruction or bypass.
Hemodynamically unstable patients should undergo open surgery.
Endovascular therapy may be used in unstable patients or those not suitable for open surgery.
Late Management: After treatment of an acute infection, at least 6 weeks of targeted antimicrobial therapy is recommended.
For infections involving resistant organisms or after reconstruction, prolonged suppressive therapy can be considered.
Additional Notes: Graft infections, although uncommon, can be influenced by factors such as contamination during implantation.

Recommendations for Atherosclerotic Dise.Recommendation: In patients with aortic atherosclerotic disease and concomitant coronary artery disease, PAD or both, it is recommended to prescribe antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulant therapy or both, guided by the clinical setting.
In patients with aortic atherosclerotic disease and risk factors for confirmed coronary artery disease, it is reasonable to prescribe a moderate- or high-intensity statin.
In patients with aortic atheromas of a thickness ≥4 mm, statin therapy may be reasonable.

content.Synopsis: Atherosclerosis is a chronic immunoinflammatory, fibroproliferative disease of the aorta. Management includes risk factor control, lifestyle modification, and pharmacological therapies.
content.Aortic Thrombus: Aortic mural thrombus is associated with underlying aortic pathology and may present with symptoms such as limb ischemia or stroke.

content.General: The guideline discusses various aortic conditions and their management strategies. It covers primary aortic thrombus management, aortic occlusion, porcelain aorta, and coarctation of the aorta. Long-term anticoagulation and surgical interventions are highlighted for thrombus management. Aortic occlusion is typically treated surgically, with options including endovascular and open surgical methods. Porcelain aorta is associated with extensive calcification, making surgeries challenging. Coarctation of the aorta involves narrowing that may lead to hypertension and requires imaging for diagnosis.
content.Aortic Thrombus: Patients with primary aortic thrombus are often managed with anticoagulation or surgical therapy. Long-term anticoagulation is usually considered to prevent embolization.
content.Aortic Occlusion: Occurs due to atherosclerotic disease and requires surgical treatment. It's essential to determine the cause and extent of the disease.
content.Porcelain Aorta: Characterized by extensive calcification and occurs in older patients with increased cardiovascular risk.
content.Coarctation of the Aorta: Involves narrowing near the left subclavian artery, often diagnosed through upper extremity hypertension.

content.CoA: Coarctation of the Aorta (CoA) may have various anatomic variants and complications such as aortic dissection, heart failure, and aneurysms. Patients often require lifelong monitoring, with evaluation methods such as TTE, MRI, and CT recommended for surveillance and follow-up. Management options include endovascular, open surgical, and hybrid procedures, influenced by patient-specific features. Hypertension, common after CoA repair, is managed through guideline-directed medical therapy, with ambulatory BP monitoring and exercise testing being useful for management.
content.BAV: Bicuspid Aortic Valve (BAV), often coexists with CoA, noted in 50% of cases.
content.ASCA: Aberrant Subclavian Artery (ASCA), may present with symptoms due to its position. Variants like Kommerell diverticulum can lead to serious complications including dissection and embolization.

key_points: TTE can detect gradients across the site of coarctation and assess for the recurrence of coarctation.
Imaging such as MRI or CT is preferable for arch and descending aortic complications.
Surveillance imaging post-CoA repair is often obtained every 3 to 5 years.
Significant narrowing or recoarctation may include collateral formation.
Upper extremity hypertension and lower extremity hypoperfusion are typical signs of CoA.
Adults with CoA are at risk for complications including hypertension and intracranial aneurysms.
Screening strategies for intracranial aneurysms require shared decision-making.

recommendations.recommendation: For patients with an Aberrant Subclavian Artery without thoracic aortic imaging, dedicated imaging to assess for TAA is reasonable.
For patients with Kommerell's diverticulum, repair might be reasonable based on anatomy and comorbidities if the diverticulum orifice is >3.0 cm.

content.Synopis: Anomalies of the aortic arch are often detected incidentally during imaging. An ASCA arises as the fourth aortic branch, affecting the trachea and esophagus. Kommerell’s diverticulum is a remnant of the aortic arch and presents in 20-60% of patients with an aberrant subclavian artery. Surgical intervention is recommended if certain diameter thresholds are exceeded.
content.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Variant aortic arch anatomy correlates significantly with thoracic aortic aneurysm (TAA) and thoracic aortic dissection (TAD).

Recommendation for Aberrant Left Vertebr.Recommendation: In patients with an aberrant left vertebral artery origin arising directly from the thoracic aorta who require aortic repair involving reconstruction or coverage of the vertebral artery origin, revascularization of the vertebral artery is reasonable.

Recommendation for Bovine Arch (Common I.Recommendation: In patients with bovine arch (common innominate and left carotid artery), imaging to assess for TAA may be reasonable.

content.Synopsis: The left vertebral artery commonly arises from the aortic arch, occurring in 6% of adults. Management involves revascularization during elective or emergency thoracic aortic repair to prevent vertebrobasilar insufficiency, SCI, and stroke. Techniques include open bypass or transposition.
content.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Preoperative revascularization of the left subclavian artery decreases the risk of stroke and SCI during elective TEVAR with planned left subclavian artery coverage. Successful outcomes were noted in a small series with aberrant left vertebral artery requiring aortic arch replacement.

content.Normal Aortic Arch Configuration: The type I aortic arch is the most common configuration, occurring in approximately 70% of adults, where the 3 great vessels originate directly from the aorta. Two types of bovine aortic arch configurations are described: Type II-A, found in 9% of the population, where the left common carotid artery arises directly from the innominate artery; and Type II-B, found in 13% of the population, where the innominate and left common carotid arteries share a common origin.
content.Bovine Arch and Aortic Expansion: A bovine aortic arch is considered a marker for thoracic aortic disease (TAD) and more rapid aortic expansion. In patients with TAD, 26.3% had a bovine arch compared to 16.4% in controls. In patients with thoracic aortic aneurysm (TAA), the annual growth rate was 0.29 cm/y in those with a bovine arch.
content.Risk and Outcomes: There is limited data suggesting that a bovine arch increases the risk of aortic dissection. In patients with acute type A aortic dissection, a bovine arch increased operative stroke risk but was not linked to worse long-term survival.
content.Tumors: Most tumors of the thoracic aorta are secondary, resulting from contiguous or metastatic spread from nearby malignancies, especially lung and esophageal cancer. Primary malignant tumors of the aorta are rare and typically manifest as sarcomas.

Recommendations.Recommendation: For patients with significant aortic disease, education and guidance should be provided about avoiding intense isometric exercises (eg, heavy weightlifting or activities requiring the Valsalva maneuver), burst exertion and activities, and collision sports.
For patients who have undergone surgery for aortic aneurysm or dissection, postoperative cardiac rehabilitation is recommended.

In patients with thoracic or abdominal aortic aneurysms whose BP is adequately controlled, it is reasonable to encourage 30 to 60 minutes of mild-to-moderate intensity aerobic activity at least 3 to 4 days per week.

For patients with clinically significant aortic disease, it is reasonable to screen for anxiety, depression, and posttraumatic stress disorder and, when indicated, provide resources for support; it is also reasonable to provide education and resources to minimize patients' concerns, support optimal decision-making, and enhance quality of life.

Additional Information.HRQOL: A focus on patient-reported health-related quality of life outcomes is becoming increasingly important. HRQOL measures include generic patient-reported outcome measures and validated aneurysm-specific measures.

content.exercise_recommendations: Exercise intensity should be individualized, considering factors like aortic pathology, aortic diameter, family history, etc. Moderate aerobic exercise may benefit aortic wall integrity.
content.cardiac_rehabilitation: Cardiac rehab is beneficial post-aortic surgery for improved peak oxygen uptake, maximal workload, and HRQOL. Low levels of resistance activities are recommended post-dissection.
content.high_intensity_athletic_training: High-intensity training is an indicator of aortic growth risk. Athlete competitiveness and aortic enlargement correlate, but mild-to-moderate intensity exercise benefits cardiovascular health.
content.psychological_health: Depression and anxiety after aortic disease surgery are common. PTSD is a particular risk and resources should be offered to address mental health.
content.HRQOL_tools: The SF-36 is used to assess patient concerns like activity restriction and family issues. Pre- and post-operative HRQOL data are needed for better decision making.
content.cost_considerations: Cost-effectiveness in AAA treatment varies by age and gender. EVAR is less costly initially but may require more expenses long-term.
content.women_vs_men: Women have lower rates of AAA but longer life expectancy, which impacts cost-effectiveness of screening and surgery.

content.cost_effectiveness: Few data exist on the cost-effectiveness of management strategies for TAA. Management of AAS is not easily modifiable.
content.specialist_follow_up: Patients with chronic TAD often see specialists including cardiologists and surgeons.
content.imaging: Diagnostic imaging is often duplicated due to differences in imaging protocols or quality. Efforts should be made to reduce redundant imaging and visits.
content.evidence_gaps: Recommendations are based on opinion and data from observational studies. More randomized trials are needed for prevention, diagnosis, and treatment.
content.biomarker_studies: Biomarkers have not been associated with events effectively. Most studies focus on protein-based and noncoding RNA biomarkers.
content.genetic_factors: Genes linked to TAA and dissection. Genetic testing can identify mutations increasing risk.
content.drug_interaction: Fluoroquinolone use is associated with an increased risk of aortic aneurysm and dissection.
content.biomechanics_of_aorta: Aortic diameter alone is insufficient for predicting dissection risk. Biomechanical wall stress understanding is crucial.
content.sex_race_ethnicity: Conflicting data exist regarding the impact of sex and race on outcomes in aortic disease patients. More research is needed.

content.Quality of Life in Patients With Aortic: Baseline HRQOL assessment in patients with aortic disease is lacking. Studies targeting HRQOL have only been conducted in patients receiving endovascular or open aortic repair. Long-term effects of interventions are unknown, particularly physical and mental HRQOL. Knowledge is narrow or limited to patients with Marfan syndrome; almost no studies have been performed with Loeys-Dietz syndrome or vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. Research is needed on ways to improve and sustain patient engagement.
content.New Endovascular Technology: Advancements in endovascular technology have significantly impacted treatment strategies in aortic disease. Current designs are limited by the differing hemodynamic and anatomic challenges in aortic anatomy. Operator experience and methodological patient selection are crucial for optimal outcomes. Continued evolution in stent-graft design is important.
content.Optimal Exercise and Rehabilitation Prot: Very limited research on optimal exercise strategies for aortic disease patients. No specific rehabilitation strategies exist post-surgical aortic repair. Development of patient-centric rehabilitation protocols is needed.
content.Equitable Care and Training Opportunitie: Sociodemographic disparities pose challenges to patients and clinicians in cardiovascular and aortic care. Optimizing care depends on regional quality improvement projects to standardize management. Physician participation in improvement programs and educational training is critical.
